attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Original grammar structure and interpretations. Synthesized from the published research of Aaron Beck (CBT), Marsha Linehan (DBT), Sue Johnson (EFT), John Bowlby (attachment), Silvan Tomkins (affect theory), Paul Ekman (basic emotions), Jeffrey Young (schema therapy), Stephen Porges (polyvagal), Peter Fonagy (mentalization), Carl Rogers (person-centered), and Carl Jung (projection/shadow). No text copied from copyrighted works.

name: The Inner Triangle — Emotion, Cognition & Behavior in Relation
description:
A 68-card oracle deck mapping the CBT triangle — Feel, Think, Do — placed in the relational field. Every emotion asks: what does your partner see? Every thought asks: what would their version be? Every behavior asks: what does it trigger across from you? 3 suits (Emotions, Cognitions, Behaviors), 14 L2 named triangles mapping specific loops (The Anxious Triangle, The Avoidant Triangle, The Shame-Hide Triangle, The Vulnerability-Connection Triangle), and 4 L3 meta-patterns. Companion grammar to Relationship Cards.

Grounded in CBT (Beck), DBT (Linehan), EFT (Johnson), attachment theory (Bowlby), affect theory (Tomkins), schema therapy (Young), polyvagal theory (Porges), and mentalization (Fonagy).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Edvard Munch's expressionist paintings of emotion — 'The Scream' (1893), 'Anxiety' (1894), 'Melancholy' (1894-96), 'Jealousy' (1895). Käthe Kollwitz's charcoal drawings of grief, tenderness, and maternal emotion (1900s-1930s). Francisco Goya's 'Black Paintings' (1819-1823) — Saturn, the dog, the witches' sabbath. William Blake's illustrations of states of being from 'Songs of Innocence and Experience' (1789-1794). Honoré Daumier's lithographs of human expression and social emotion (1830s-1870s). Charles Le Brun's 'Conference on Expression' (1668) — systematic drawings of facial expressions mapping emotion to physiology.

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] Tomkins' anger-rage affect. The boundary-protector. Anger says: something that matters to me has been violated. Evolutionary function: mobilization against threat. Not pathological — NECESSARY. Linehan: anger is a valid emotion that contains information. The question isn't 'should I feel angry?' but 'what is my anger telling me?' Ekman: one of 6 universal emotions, recognized across all cultures. Darwin: anger readies the body for confrontation — the oldest survival tool.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] What your partner sees: a raised voice, a sharp tone, a closed face, crossed arms. What's underneath: 'Something important to me was violated and I need you to see it.' The gap between surface (scary) and depth (hurt) is where most relationship damage happens. Johnson: anger in couples is almost always attachment protest — 'I matter to you, RIGHT?' Expressed skillfully: anger clears the air. Expressed destructively: it becomes the Four Horsemen.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Chronic anger: everything is a threat. Rage as default. Anger that can't be expressed becomes depression (Freud) or somatization (tension, headaches, gut problems). Anger used as power: intimidation, control. Also: anger turned inward — self-blame, self-harm, autoimmune flare. The shadow of suppressed anger: passive aggression, sarcasm, cold withdrawal.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Clean anger: 'I feel angry because X matters to me and it was violated.' The mobilizing force that sets boundaries, demands justice, protects the vulnerable. Linehan: anger is information — it tells you what your values are. Healthy anger expressed to a partner who can receive it DEEPENS intimacy. It says: I trust you enough to show you this.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Jaw clenches. Fists tighten. Heat rises — face, neck, chest. Heart rate spikes (Gottman: above 100 BPM = flooding). Shallow breathing. Forward-leaning posture. The sympathetic nervous system's fight response. Darwin: 'The body of a man or animal under the excitement of anger is commonly described as one from which the blood has mounted to the head.'
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] What triggered your anger? What value or boundary was violated? What does your partner see when you're angry — and what's actually underneath? Can you say 'I'm angry because...' without attacking? Where do you feel anger in your body right now?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] Tomkins' distress-anguish affect. The letting-go emotion. Sadness says: something has been lost. A person, a hope, a version of yourself, a future you imagined. Evolutionary function: signaling the need for comfort, slowing down to process loss. Distinct from depression (which is the ABSENCE of feeling) — sadness is fully alive, fully present grief. Bowlby: sadness is the natural response to broken attachment. It calls others toward us.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Sadness shown is a powerful bid for connection — IF the partner can receive it. Johnson: when one partner weeps and the other holds them, the bond deepens. But many couples can't do this. The sad partner hides it ('I don't want to be a burden'). The witnessing partner tries to FIX it ('Don't cry, it'll be okay') instead of just being WITH it. The missed sadness becomes loneliness.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Chronic sadness as identity: 'I'm the sad one.' Sadness weaponized: tears used to end arguments or avoid accountability. Sadness suppressed: 'Big boys don't cry,' 'Be strong.' Suppressed sadness becomes numbness, irritability, or physical illness. Also: sadness that overwhelms — grief without a container, without co-regulation, without end.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Sadness that is FELT and SHARED. The cathartic cry. The grief that processes loss and makes room for what comes next. Sadness shared with a partner = one of the deepest forms of intimacy. It says: 'I trust you with my most tender place.' Winnicott: the capacity for sadness is a sign of emotional health — it means you can love deeply enough to grieve.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Heaviness in the chest. Throat tightens (the lump). Eyes sting. Energy drops — the body wants to curl inward, lie down, slow down. Breathing deepens, slows. Parasympathetic activation: the body is saying 'stop, process, rest.' Tears release cortisol and endorphins — crying literally helps regulate the nervous system.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] What have you lost or are you grieving — even something small? Can you show your sadness to your partner, or do you hide it? When your partner is sad, do you try to fix or just be present? Where does sadness live in your body right now?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] Tomkins' fear-terror affect. The protector. Fear says: danger is present or approaching. Porges: neuroception scans for threat BEFORE conscious awareness — you feel afraid before you know why. Evolutionary function: survival. The most primal emotion, hardwired deepest. In relationships, fear usually wears a mask: it shows up as anger, control, avoidance, or criticism. Johnson: beneath every angry pursuer and every shut-down withdrawer, there is fear — fear of loss, fear of inadequacy, fear of abandonment.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] The hidden driver. Fear of abandonment → pursuit. Fear of engulfment → withdrawal. Fear of vulnerability → walls. Fear of being seen → performing. Most couple fights are fear arguing with fear, but neither partner names it. Johnson's key move: 'Can you tell your partner what you're afraid of right now?' When fear is named instead of acted out, the partner's response shifts from defense to tenderness.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Chronic fear: hypervigilance, anxiety, inability to rest. Fear that becomes controlling: checking, monitoring, restricting the partner's freedom. Fear projected: 'YOU'RE the one who can't be trusted.' Fear frozen: the person who can't take any risks because every possibility contains catastrophe. Porges: chronic sympathetic or dorsal vagal activation — the body stuck in threat mode.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Fear as wisdom. The gut feeling that says 'something's wrong here.' Fear that protects boundaries, prevents genuine danger, keeps children safe. And: the courage that comes from FEELING the fear and acting anyway. Johnson: the softening moment IS fear spoken aloud — 'I'm terrified of losing you' — and it's the bravest thing one partner can say to another.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Heart pounds. Palms sweat. Stomach drops. Muscles tense for flight. Peripheral vision narrows (tunnel vision). Breathing becomes shallow and fast. Porges: sympathetic activation — adrenaline flooding the system. In chronic fear: insomnia, digestive problems, tension headaches. The body is running a threat program 24/7.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] What are you afraid of in your relationship right now? Does your fear show up as anger, control, avoidance, or something else? What would happen if you said 'I'm scared' instead of what you usually say? Can your partner handle your fear — or would naming it feel too dangerous?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] Tomkins' enjoyment-joy affect. The expansion emotion. Joy says: something good is happening RIGHT NOW — be present for it. Not happiness (which is a judgment about life) but joy (which is a body state of aliveness). Ekman: one of 6 universals. Fredrickson's broaden-and-build: joy doesn't just feel good, it literally expands perception, creativity, and social connection. Porges: joy requires ventral vagal safety — you can only feel joy when your nervous system says 'safe.'
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Joy shared is joy doubled — but only if the partner can receive it. Gottman's 'turning toward': when one partner shares good news and the other responds with genuine enthusiasm (active-constructive responding), the bond strengthens. The killer: when joy is met with dismissal ('that's nice'), jealousy, or one-upping. Many couples lose joy not through conflict but through the slow erosion of shared delight. Also: joy that excludes — one partner's joy that the other can't access.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Joy avoidance: the person who can't let themselves feel good because they're waiting for the other shoe to drop (foreboding joy — Brené Brown). Joy as mania: forced positivity, toxic optimism, refusing to feel anything else. Joy weaponized: 'I'm FINE, why can't YOU be happy?' Numbing to joy: when depression or chronic stress flattens the capacity for positive emotion. Also: couples who lost their joy and can't remember ever having it.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Spontaneous, unforced delight. The laugh that takes you by surprise. Joy in small things — the morning coffee ritual, the inside joke, the shared sunset. Gottman: the 5:1 ratio works because joy is the MAIN CURRENCY of healthy relationships, not the absence of conflict. Joy that includes the other. Play, silliness, lightness. The couple who can still make each other laugh after 30 years.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Chest opens. Face lifts — the genuine Duchenne smile (eyes crinkle). Energy rises but softly — not the spike of anger but the glow of warmth. Breathing deepens naturally. Muscles relax. Laughter engages the diaphragm, releases endorphins, synchronizes breathing between partners. Porges: ventral vagal activation — the social engagement system fully online. The body says 'yes.'
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] When was the last time you felt genuine joy with your partner? What small things used to bring you delight together? When your partner shares something good, how do you respond — really? Do you let yourself feel joy, or do you brace against it? What would it take to play together again?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] Tomkins' disgust and dissmell affects. The rejection emotion. Disgust says: this is toxic — push it away. Originally evolved for food rejection (Darwin: the wrinkled nose, the protruding tongue). Ekman: universal, cross-cultural. In relationships, disgust is contempt's raw material. Rozin: disgust expanded from physical (spoiled food) to moral (violated values) to interpersonal ('they disgust me'). When you feel disgust toward a partner, something fundamental has shifted — you've moved from 'their behavior is bad' to 'THEY are bad.'
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Gottman's most dangerous horseman is contempt, and contempt is disgust directed at a person's character. When one partner feels disgust, the other senses it instantly — in the curled lip, the eye roll, the involuntary recoil. Disgust is almost impossible to hide and almost impossible to receive. The partner who feels disgusted often doesn't know it yet — they just feel 'turned off' or 'done.' The partner who is the object of disgust feels it in their bones.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Disgust as dehumanization. When a partner becomes 'disgusting' rather than 'someone doing something I dislike,' the relationship is in crisis (Gottman: contempt is the #1 predictor of divorce). Disgust that generalizes: from one behavior to the whole person. Self-disgust: 'I'm repulsive' — internalized shame wearing the mask of disgust. Moral disgust weaponized: 'You SHOULD be disgusted with yourself.'
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Disgust as a values compass. The gut reaction that says 'this crosses a line' is valuable information — it protects boundaries, identifies genuine violations, signals when something is truly wrong (not just uncomfortable). Clean disgust: 'I feel revolted by what happened' (the act, not the person). Disgust that motivates change rather than contempt.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] The nose wrinkles. Upper lip curls. Stomach contracts — nausea, gagging reflex. The body literally tries to expel. Darwin: 'The term disgust in its simplest sense means something offensive to the taste.' Shallow breathing through the mouth (avoiding the smell). Physical recoil — leaning back, turning away. In chronic disgust toward a partner: loss of sexual desire, aversion to touch.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] Have you felt disgust toward your partner — even fleetingly? Is your disgust about a specific behavior or about them as a person? When did 'I don't like what you did' become 'I don't like who you are'? Do you feel disgust toward yourself? What boundary is your disgust trying to protect?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] Tomkins' surprise-startle affect. The reset emotion. Surprise says: your model of reality was just invalidated — update NOW. The briefest of all affects — it lasts a fraction of a second before giving way to whatever comes next (fear, joy, anger, curiosity). Ekman: the only truly neutral emotion — it can go positive or negative depending on what caused it. Evolutionary function: interrupt ongoing processing, orient to new information. In relationships, surprise is the gateway to either discovery or threat.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Surprise reveals how safe the relationship is. When surprised by a partner, the immediate follow-on emotion tells you everything: 'Oh!' → delight (safe) vs. 'Oh!' → dread (unsafe). Long-term couples stop surprising each other — the Love Map becomes fixed, curiosity dies. Gottman: keeping the Love Map updated requires ongoing curiosity about who your partner is BECOMING, not just who they were. The death of surprise is the death of aliveness in the bond.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Surprise avoidance: the need to control everything so nothing unexpected happens. Hypervigilance dressed as planning. Also: the partner who uses surprise as power — unpredictable moods, sudden reversals, keeping the other off-balance (a hallmark of emotional abuse). Shock that overwhelms: betrayal, discovery of secrets, the revelation that your model of the relationship was wrong.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] The delight of being surprised by your partner — they're MORE than you thought. The 'I didn't know that about you' moment that reignites curiosity. Surprise as play: spontaneity, gifts, breaking routine. Also: the surprise of growth — watching your partner become someone new and letting your model update. Couples who can say 'you surprised me' with wonder, not fear.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] The startle response: eyebrows shoot up, eyes widen, mouth opens, body briefly freezes. Heart rate spikes momentarily. Orienting reflex — head turns toward the source. The whole body says 'WHAT?' for a split second before the thinking mind catches up. Darwin: the startle is involuntary and identical across cultures. Tomkins: surprise-startle is the 'reset button' of the affect system.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] When was the last time your partner genuinely surprised you? Was the surprise welcome or threatening? Do you allow yourself to be surprised, or do you need to predict everything? What would it feel like to discover something new about your partner this week? Does surprise feel like adventure or danger in your body?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] Tomkins' shame-humiliation affect. The hiding emotion. Shame says: I am fundamentally flawed — not 'I did a bad thing' (that's guilt) but 'I AM bad.' The self turns against the self. Linehan: shame is the emotion of perceived defectiveness. Brené Brown: shame is the intensely painful feeling that we are unworthy of love and belonging. Evolutionary function: social conformity, maintaining group bonds (but at devastating personal cost). The most isolating emotion — shame tells you to hide, which cuts you off from the connection that could heal it.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Shame is the silent destroyer of intimacy. The partner carrying shame can't be fully seen because being seen feels dangerous — if you really knew me, you'd leave. Johnson: shame blocks the softening moves in EFT — the person can't say 'I need you' because needing feels pathetic. Shame makes people perform rather than connect. And: shame induced BY the partner — the eye roll, the contempt, the 'what's wrong with you?' that confirms the worst fear. Many couples cycles are shame bouncing between two people.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Chronic shame: identity organized around defectiveness. 'I am the problem.' Shame that becomes rage: narcissistic injury — attacking others to avoid feeling small. Shame that becomes control: perfectionism, never making mistakes, needing to be RIGHT. Shame spirals: feeling ashamed of feeling ashamed. Toxic shame (Bradshaw): the shame that was downloaded in childhood and runs the operating system without conscious awareness.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Healthy shame: the signal that you've violated your own values (close to guilt). The humility that keeps ego in check. But the real light of shame is what happens when it's SHARED: when one partner says 'I feel ashamed' and the other responds with tenderness instead of confirmation, shame loses its power. Brown: shame cannot survive being spoken. Johnson: the moment a partner shares their shame and is met with compassion is the deepest healing move in therapy.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] The body collapses inward. Shoulders round. Eyes drop. Face flushes or goes pale. The urge to disappear, shrink, become invisible. Tomkins: shame interrupts the enjoyment-joy affect — the face that was open and bright suddenly falls. Heat in the face and chest. Nausea. The physical sensation of wanting the ground to swallow you. Chronic shame: collapsed posture becomes habitual, voice becomes quiet, eye contact becomes difficult.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] What are you most ashamed of — the thing you've never told your partner? Does shame show up as hiding, performing, or attacking in your relationship? When your partner sees a part of you that you're ashamed of, what do you do? Has your partner ever shamed you — intentionally or not? What would it feel like to be fully seen and still accepted?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The repair emotion. Guilt says: I did something that violated my values or hurt someone I care about — and I want to make it right. Crucially different from shame: guilt is about BEHAVIOR ('I did a bad thing'), shame is about SELF ('I am bad'). Tangney: guilt is the prosocial emotion — it motivates repair, apology, and changed behavior. Linehan: guilt is appropriate when it fits the facts and motivates effective action. Evolutionary function: maintaining social bonds through accountability.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Guilt is the engine of repair in relationships. Gottman: successful couples don't avoid hurting each other — they feel guilt when they do and they repair. The partner who can say 'I feel bad about what I did' is offering a gift. But guilt only works if the other partner can RECEIVE the repair. Johnson: in rigid cycles, guilt gets stuck — one partner carries all of it (the over-guilty one) while the other carries none (the defended one). Healthy guilt is proportionate, temporary, and leads to action.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Chronic guilt: feeling responsible for everything, including things that aren't your fault. The person who apologizes for existing. Guilt as manipulation: 'Look how bad I feel' becomes a way to avoid consequences or shift the focus to the guilty person's pain. Guilt that blocks change: so consumed by feeling bad that you never actually DO differently. Also: absent guilt — the inability to feel remorse, which signals a deeper disconnection from empathy or attachment.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Clean guilt: 'I hurt you. I see it. I'm sorry. Here's what I'll do differently.' The most powerful repair sequence in couples therapy. Guilt that is proportionate to the offense, leads to genuine apology, and results in changed behavior. Tangney: guilt predicts empathy, perspective-taking, and constructive responses to anger. The couple where both partners can feel and express appropriate guilt has the foundation for lasting repair.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Heaviness in the chest — a weight. Stomach discomfort. The impulse to approach (unlike shame, which says hide, guilt says go toward and fix). Restlessness — the body wants to DO something. Difficulty sleeping. Replaying the scene. The physical experience of conscience: the body registering that something needs to be made right. In chronic guilt: exhaustion, tension, the body carrying a burden that never lifts.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] What do you feel guilty about in your relationship right now? Is your guilt proportionate — or do you carry guilt for things that aren't your fault? When you feel guilty, do you apologize and change, or just feel bad? Does your partner make space for your repair, or hold onto your offense? Is there something you SHOULD feel guilty about but don't?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The signal of unmet attachment need. Loneliness says: I am not held, not seen, not reached. Not the same as being alone — you can be lonely in a crowded room, lonely in a marriage, lonely beside a partner who is physically present but emotionally gone. Johnson: loneliness in a couple is the most painful loneliness of all, because the person who is supposed to be your safe haven IS the source of the ache. Cacioppo: chronic loneliness is as dangerous as smoking 15 cigarettes a day — the body treats social disconnection as a survival threat.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] The loneliness that lives inside a relationship is often invisible. Both partners can feel it simultaneously without either naming it. Johnson: the 'raw spots' in EFT — moments when one partner reaches and the other isn't there, and the absence echoes all the way back to childhood. Loneliness expressed becomes a bid for connection. Loneliness hidden becomes withdrawal, resentment, or an affair. Many couples in therapy aren't angry — they're lonely.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Loneliness that becomes identity: 'I will always be alone, even with someone.' Loneliness that leads to desperate measures — affairs, substance use, workaholism — anything to fill the void. Loneliness weaponized: 'You MAKE me lonely' (blaming the partner for an internal state). Also: the loneliness of the pursuing partner who can't stop reaching, and the loneliness of the withdrawing partner who can't let anyone in.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Loneliness felt and named is already less lonely — it contains within it the knowledge that connection is possible and desired. The moment one partner says 'I feel lonely with you' and the other hears it without defending, the loneliness begins to dissolve. Johnson: A.R.E. — Are you Accessible, Responsive, Engaged? Loneliness is the alarm that says A.R.E. is missing. The alarm works. It calls both partners back.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Ache in the chest — literally, heartache. Cold sensation. The body pulls inward, arms might self-hug. Heaviness without the release of tears. Cacioppo: loneliness triggers the same neural pain circuits as physical injury. Sleep disruption — the lonely body doesn't fully rest. Cortisol elevation. The immune system weakens. The body knows: isolation is danger.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] Do you feel lonely in your relationship right now? Is there a difference between being alone and being lonely for you? When you feel lonely with your partner, what do you do — reach, withdraw, or pretend it's fine? Have you told your partner you feel lonely? What would 'not lonely' feel like in your body?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The reaching emotion. Longing says: there is something I want deeply — a closeness, a version of us, a feeling I once had — and I can feel the distance between here and there. Distinct from wanting (which is cognitive) — longing is in the body, a pull, an ache-toward. Bowlby: proximity-seeking is the most fundamental attachment behavior. Longing is proximity-seeking as felt experience. Tagore: 'The song I came to sing remains unsung.' Longing holds the memory or the vision of connection and measures the gap.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Longing is what couples in distress often feel beneath their anger. The pursuing partner longs for closeness and shows it as demand. The withdrawing partner longs for acceptance and shows it as distance. Johnson: when longing is spoken directly — 'I miss you, I miss us, I want to feel close again' — it cuts through every defensive pattern. Longing shared is the beginning of re-engagement. But longing unspoken becomes resentment, nostalgia for an idealized past, or resignation.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Longing for a partner who doesn't exist: the idealized version, the early-relationship version, the fantasy. Longing that becomes obsession: inability to let go, stalking, clinging. Longing as avoidance of grief: refusing to accept what's actually lost. Also: longing for a different life, a different partner, a road not taken — the grass-is-greener longing that erodes commitment without offering real alternatives.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Longing as compass: it points toward what matters most. The couple who can feel longing for each other WHILE TOGETHER is paradoxically in the most alive place — they want MORE of what they have. Perel: desire requires a gap; longing is the creative space where desire lives. Healthy longing motivates reaching, risking, trying again. It says: this matters enough to ache for.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] A pull in the chest — toward. Arms may unconsciously reach. The breath deepens as if trying to draw the other closer. Warmth mixed with ache. The body leans forward. Eyes soften. Unlike fear (which contracts) or anger (which pushes), longing OPENS the body toward the object of desire. In chronic unmet longing: the ache becomes a low hum, always present, sometimes mistaken for depression.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] What do you long for in your relationship that you're not getting? Is your longing for something real or something idealized? Can you say 'I miss you' or 'I want more of us' directly? When you feel longing, does it make you reach toward your partner or pull away? What would it be like if your longing were fully met?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The threat-to-bond emotion. Jealousy says: someone or something is threatening my connection with you. A compound emotion — fear (of loss) + anger (at the threat) + sometimes shame (am I not enough?). Evolutionary: mate-guarding behavior, present across primates. Distinct from envy (wanting what someone else has) — jealousy is about protecting what you have. Bowlby: jealousy is an attachment alarm — it fires when the primary bond feels threatened by a rival, whether real or imagined.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Jealousy is information about attachment security — or insecurity. In secure relationships, jealousy is mild and communicable: 'I noticed I felt jealous when...' In insecure relationships, jealousy becomes controlling: checking phones, restricting friendships, interrogating. Johnson: jealousy often masks the deeper fear — 'Am I your primary person?' When that question is answered clearly and consistently, jealousy quiets. When it's left ambiguous, jealousy escalates.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Possessiveness: jealousy that becomes ownership — 'You're MINE.' Paranoid jealousy: seeing threats everywhere, interpreting every interaction as betrayal. Jealousy as control: isolating the partner from friends, monitoring, demanding constant reassurance that is never enough. Retroactive jealousy: torment about the partner's past. Also: jealousy that is JUSTIFIED but dismissed — the partner who gaslights: 'You're just being jealous' when the boundary violation is real.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Jealousy as a signal that the bond matters. Mild jealousy, felt and shared, can actually increase desire and commitment — it reminds both partners that they CHOSE each other and could lose each other. Healthy jealousy is proportionate, communicable, and responsive to reassurance. It says: 'I value us enough to notice when something threatens us.' The couple who can talk about jealousy without shame or rage has a strong alliance.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Heat in the chest and face. Stomach churns. Jaw tightens. Eyes narrow — vigilance, scanning for the rival. Adrenaline: the body prepares for competition or confrontation. Heart rate elevates. The whole body says 'MINE' — a primate response millions of years old. In chronic jealousy: cortisol stays elevated, sleep disrupted, intrusive images, the body in a state of constant threat surveillance.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] What triggers jealousy in you — and is the trigger proportionate to the threat? Can you tell your partner 'I felt jealous' without it becoming an accusation? Is your jealousy about the present situation or an old wound? When your partner expresses jealousy, do you reassure or dismiss? What would 'secure enough to not be jealous' feel like?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The slow-burn emotion. Resentment says: I've been hurt, it wasn't addressed, and I'm keeping score. Not a primary emotion but a COMPOUND: anger + hurt + time. Resentment is what happens when legitimate grievances go unacknowledged or unrepaired. Gottman: resentment is the accumulated sediment of failed repair attempts. Each unrepaired injury adds a brick to the wall. Unlike anger (which is hot and immediate), resentment is cold and cumulative — it builds quietly until the wall is too high to see over.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Resentment is the relationship's accounting ledger — and when it grows unbalanced, everything becomes a transaction. 'I did the dishes, you didn't, AGAIN.' The resentful partner keeps score because the scores were never settled. Gottman: Negative Sentiment Override — when resentment reaches a threshold, the partner's neutral or even positive actions get interpreted negatively. Everything proves the case. The non-resentful partner feels they can never win. Both are right.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Resentment that becomes the relationship's identity: 'We're the couple that fights about chores.' Resentment that masks the real issue: the argument isn't about the dishes, it's about 'Do you value me? Do you see what I contribute?' Passive aggression: the covert expression of resentment — compliance on the surface, sabotage underneath. Martyr resentment: 'I give and give and you never appreciate it.' The bitterness that poisons everything, including joy.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Resentment as diagnostic: it tells you EXACTLY where the unfinished business lives. Every resentment contains an unmet need and an undelivered message. When resentment is unpacked — 'I resent that because what I actually needed was...' — it becomes the roadmap for repair. Gottman: processing a regrettable incident requires naming the underlying need. Resentment holds the need like a fossil in rock. Break the rock open.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Chronic tension: shoulders, jaw, gut. The body carries the ledger. Tight lips — the held-back words. A heaviness that's different from sadness — it's weighted with injustice. Shallow breathing. The body braces slightly whenever the partner is present. Sleep disturbance from ruminating. Over time: headaches, digestive problems, back pain. The body becomes the storage system for every unresolved hurt.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] What are you resentful about — the surface issue or the deeper need? Have you told your partner what's building up, or are you keeping score silently? Is your resentment about current behavior or accumulated history? What repair would actually address the wound underneath? If you let go of the resentment, what would you feel instead?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The superiority emotion. Contempt says: I am above you. Not 'I'm angry at what you did' but 'I look DOWN on who you are.' Ekman added contempt as the 7th universal emotion — the only asymmetric facial expression (one side of the lip curls up). Gottman: the single greatest predictor of divorce. Contempt is disgust + moral judgment + superiority. It differs from anger (which treats the other as an equal adversary) in that it treats the partner as BENEATH engagement. The contemptuous partner has already left the relationship emotionally.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Gottman's research: couples who express contempt divorce at rates above 90%. Contempt communicates: 'You are worthless.' It attacks the partner's sense of self, not their behavior. The eye roll, the sneer, the mocking tone, the sarcasm that drips with superiority. Johnson: contempt is the opposite of A.R.E. — it says 'I am not accessible, not responsive, not engaged, and you don't DESERVE me to be.' The partner who receives contempt is being told they are fundamentally defective.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Contempt is ITSELF the shadow — it's the most destructive relational emotion. But it has its own shadow: contempt that masks vulnerability. The contemptuous partner often feels inadequate themselves and uses superiority as armor. Contempt bred by family: 'My family was better than yours.' Cultural contempt: classism, racism, sexism expressed within the couple. Self-contempt: the internal voice of 'you're pathetic' that gets projected outward.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] The light of contempt is its dissolution — the moment you realize you're looking DOWN at your partner and choose to look ACROSS instead. The recognition that contempt is a defense against your own vulnerability. Gottman: the antidote to contempt is building a culture of appreciation — actively scanning for what your partner does RIGHT. Every expression of genuine gratitude is a brick removed from the contempt wall. The humble realization: I am not above this person. We are in this together.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] The unilateral lip curl — Ekman's contempt micro-expression. Head tilts back slightly (looking down the nose). Arms may cross. The body creates DISTANCE and HEIGHT — both at once. Breathing becomes controlled, dismissive sigh. The body doesn't prepare for engagement (like anger) — it prepares for disengagement. Chronic contempt: the body carries a perpetual stance of superiority that the person may not consciously notice but their partner feels in every interaction.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] Have you felt contempt for your partner — even for a moment? When you criticize, do you attack the behavior or the person? Do you feel superior to your partner in any domain? When was the last time you expressed genuine appreciation? Is there self-contempt underneath your contempt for your partner?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The openness emotion. Vulnerability says: I am exposed, undefended, and willing to be seen. Not weakness — the opposite: vulnerability requires more courage than any defense. Brown: vulnerability is the birthplace of love, belonging, creativity, and joy. Johnson: every EFT softening move IS a vulnerability move — dropping the armor, showing the soft underbelly. It's both an emotional state (feeling exposed) and a relational act (choosing to show it). The prerequisite for genuine intimacy.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Johnson: the softening event — when one partner drops their defensive position and shows their vulnerability — is THE change event in couple therapy. 'I push you away because I'm terrified you'll see how much I need you' is a vulnerability that transforms the dynamic. But vulnerability requires a receiving partner: if I show my soft place and you attack it, the wound is catastrophic. Trust = 'I can be vulnerable with you and you will handle it with care.' Betrayal = 'I was vulnerable with you and you used it against me.'
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Forced vulnerability: pressuring a partner to 'open up' before they're ready. Vulnerability as manipulation: performing openness to gain sympathy or control. Vulnerability without boundaries: sharing everything with everyone (that's not vulnerability, it's flooding). Also: the inability to be vulnerable — the armored person who CANNOT let anyone in, for whom exposure feels like annihilation. And: vulnerability exploited — the partner who catalogues your vulnerable moments and weaponizes them in future fights.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Chosen vulnerability: 'I'm going to tell you something scary because you matter more than my fear.' The moment one partner truly shows themselves and the other receives it with tenderness — this is the deepest form of human connection. Brown: 'Vulnerability is not winning or losing; it's having the courage to show up and be seen when you have no control over the outcome.' Couples who can be mutually vulnerable have everything they need.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] The body opens: chest exposed, arms uncrossed, posture softens. Breathing becomes shaky or deeper. Eyes may fill with tears. The voice quiets, slows. A trembling quality — the body registering the risk. Porges: vulnerability is only possible in ventral vagal safety — the nervous system must read 'safe enough' before the armor comes off. The physical sensation of vulnerability is almost indistinguishable from the physical sensation of intimacy.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] When was the last time you let yourself be truly vulnerable with your partner? What's the scariest thing you could share? Is it safe to be vulnerable in your relationship — really? Has your vulnerability ever been used against you? What would it take for you to show the thing you're most afraid to show?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The care emotion. Tenderness says: I see your fragility and I want to protect it. The feeling a parent has looking at a sleeping child, a partner has watching the other struggle and wanting to help without fixing. Tomkins: tenderness is related to the caregiving system — distinct from romantic love or attachment-seeking. It's the emotion that makes you gentle. Rogers: unconditional positive regard has this quality — seeing the other as inherently worthy of care. Tenderness is love with its hands open.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Tenderness is what couples lose first and miss most. The small gesture: a hand on the back, a blanket brought without being asked, the softened voice when the partner is struggling. Johnson: in the EFT softening, the RECEIVING partner's tenderness is as important as the vulnerable partner's disclosure — 'I see you, I'm here, you're safe with me.' Gottman: fondness and admiration are the tenderness system in action. When tenderness dies, the relationship becomes transactional.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Tenderness withheld as punishment: the cold partner who COULD be gentle but chooses not to. Tenderness that becomes patronizing: 'Poor baby' — the one-up position disguised as care. Tenderness that suffocates: overprotection, treating the partner as fragile, not allowing them to struggle and grow. Also: the person who can receive tenderness from others but cannot feel it toward their partner — the tenderness has dried up, and they don't know when or why.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Spontaneous tenderness: the wave of warmth that comes unbidden when you look at your partner. The brush of a hand, the gentle tone, the moment of 'I'm lucky to know this person.' Tenderness after conflict: the repair isn't just words — it's the return of gentleness. Tenderness in difficulty: caring for a sick partner, holding space during grief, the quiet acts that say 'your wellbeing is as real to me as my own.' Tenderness is love made physical and daily.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] The body softens everywhere at once. Breathing slows and deepens. Hands want to touch gently. Eyes moisten (not crying, just the softening). Face relaxes, especially around the eyes and mouth. The voice drops lower, becomes quieter. Porges: tenderness IS the ventral vagal system expressed interpersonally — the social engagement system in its most nurturing mode. Oxytocin release. The body of tenderness mirrors the body of vulnerability but from the giving side.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] When was the last time you felt tender toward your partner? Can you be tender without being asked? Do you receive tenderness easily, or does it make you uncomfortable? Has tenderness been lost between you — and when did it go? What small act of tenderness could you offer today?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The wanting-toward emotion. Desire says: I want to move closer, I want more, I want YOU. Broader than sexual desire — includes the desire to be known, to merge, to lose yourself in another. Perel: desire and security exist in fundamental tension — we want closeness AND autonomy, safety AND excitement. The paradox of long-term love: the very security that makes love possible can extinguish the desire that makes love vital. Desire requires a gap — you can't desire what you already fully possess.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Perel's central question: can desire survive domesticity? Many couples lose desire not because of failure but because of success — they built such a comfortable home together that there's no space for the unknown. Desire needs mystery, separateness, risk. Johnson would counter: desire also needs safety — the safety to be erotically vulnerable. The tension is real. Couples who maintain desire find ways to hold BOTH: deep security AND periodic strangeness. They see each other as both known and unknowable.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Desire that becomes compulsive: pornography addiction, serial affairs, the inability to be satisfied. Desire as avoidance: pursuing sexual novelty to avoid emotional intimacy. Desire withheld as punishment: the partner who controls through withdrawal of sexual or emotional availability. The death of desire: the couple who loves each other but cannot want each other — they've become roommates, siblings, co-parents, but not lovers.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Desire as the life force of the relationship — the ongoing 'I choose you' that is renewed through wanting. The couple who can desire each other AFTER seeing each other at their worst — that's a resilient desire. Perel: erotic desire includes intellectual curiosity, aesthetic appreciation, playfulness. The light of desire is when it coexists with deep knowing: 'I know you fully and I still want more of you.' Desire as renewal.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Warmth spreading through the body. Heart rate increases. Breathing quickens. Pupils dilate. The body moves toward — leaning in, reaching, seeking proximity. Dopamine surge: the reward-anticipation circuit activating. The body becomes more alert, more alive, more sensitized to touch. In the absence of desire: flatness, the body doesn't respond to the partner's presence, touch feels neutral rather than charged.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] Do you desire your partner — not just love them, but WANT them? What does desire feel like in your body? Has your desire changed over the course of your relationship — and when? Do you desire to be desired? What would reignite the wanting between you? Is there enough separateness in your relationship for desire to breathe?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The absence emotion — or rather, the emotion ABOUT the absence of emotion. Numbness says: I've shut down to survive. Porges: dorsal vagal shutdown — the nervous system's oldest defense, the 'playing dead' response when fight and flight have failed. Not a choice but a biological protection. Distinct from calm (which is alive and present) — numbness is the absence of aliveness. Linehan: emotional numbness often follows chronic invalidation — if your emotions were punished or ignored enough times, the system learns to stop producing them.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] The numb partner is often misread as 'not caring' — but they care so much that their system shut down to manage it. Johnson: the withdrawer in EFT is often numb, not indifferent. They went to their emotional pilot light because full flame was too dangerous. Their partner, usually the pursuer, reads the numbness as absence and escalates — which drives the numb partner deeper into shutdown. The cruelest cycle: the more one partner pursues feeling, the more the other partner loses access to it.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Chronic numbness: living life at 20% volume. Alexithymia — the inability to identify or describe emotions. Numbness maintained through substance use, overwork, screen time — anything to avoid the feelings that might return if the system reboots. The numb person who hurts their partner and genuinely cannot feel remorse — not because they're cruel but because the feeling system is offline. Also: numbness after trauma — dissociation as the body's emergency shutdown.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Numbness recognized is the beginning of thawing. The first crack: 'I don't feel anything and that scares me' — that fear IS a feeling, and it means the system is trying to come back online. Porges: the shift from dorsal vagal to ventral vagal requires SAFETY first — not 'try harder to feel' but 'create conditions where feeling is safe.' The partner who can sit with a numb partner WITHOUT demanding they feel something is providing the exact medicine needed.
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Everything quiet. Heart rate slow, flat. Breathing shallow but stable. Muscles neither tense nor relaxed — just... absent. The face becomes mask-like. The body feels heavy, distant, as if observed from outside. Porges: dorsal vagal activation — the metabolic conservation response. Digestion slows. Temperature may drop. The body is running on minimal power. In extreme cases: depersonalization, feeling like you're watching yourself from across the room.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] Do you feel numb right now — and is that unusual or your baseline? When did the numbness start? What was happening when you could last feel? Does your partner know you're numb, or do they think you don't care? What would it take for your nervous system to feel safe enough to turn the feelings back on?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-1: EMOTIONS] The too-much emotion. Overwhelm says: more is coming in than I can process. Siegel's window of tolerance: when arousal exceeds the window's upper edge, you're in overwhelm — hyperaroused, flooded, unable to think clearly. Gottman: 'flooding' — when heart rate exceeds ~100 BPM during conflict, rational thought goes offline and the conversation is over. Linehan: overwhelm is what happens when emotional intensity exceeds distress tolerance capacity. Not a single emotion but a STATE — too many emotions at once, too much intensity, the system overloaded.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-1] Gottman's flooding research: when one partner is physiologically overwhelmed (HR > 100 BPM), they literally cannot process their partner's words — the prefrontal cortex goes offline. Continuing to argue with a flooded partner is worse than useless — it does active damage. Johnson: overwhelm in the pursuing partner looks like escalation; overwhelm in the withdrawing partner looks like shutdown. Both are saying 'I can't.' The couple's task: learn to recognize overwhelm in yourself AND your partner, and STOP.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-1] Chronic overwhelm: the person who is always drowning, always in crisis. Overwhelm as avoidance: 'I can't deal with this right now' becomes 'I can never deal with this.' Overwhelm as weapon: dramatic emotional flooding that shuts down all conversation and forces the partner into caretaking. Also: the person who never acknowledges overwhelm, pushes through, and then explodes — the pressure cooker without a valve. Burnout: overwhelm that became a lifestyle.
sections.Light: [SESSION-1] Overwhelm recognized is overwhelm that can be managed. Gottman: the 20-minute break — when flooding is detected, take a structured break (not avoidance but regulation). Linehan: distress tolerance skills — riding the wave rather than being swept away. The couple who can say 'I'm flooded, I need 20 minutes' and the partner who can hear that as self-care rather than rejection — this is mastery. Overwhelm is the system saying 'I care about this so much that it's exceeding my capacity.'
sections.Body: [SESSION-1] Heart racing. Breathing fast and shallow. Sweating. Mind spinning — unable to focus on any single thought. Muscles tight everywhere. Nausea. Tunnel vision or scattered vision. Porges: sympathetic hyperactivation pushing toward dorsal vagal collapse — the body bouncing between fight/flight and freeze. Gottman's biofeedback: in flooding, multiple physiological systems are simultaneously dysregulated. The body is in emergency mode. The antidote is PHYSIOLOGICAL: slow breathing, cold water on the face, movement, time.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-1] What overwhelms you most in your relationship — conflict, demands, emotions, decisions? How does your partner know you're flooded? Do you have a signal or phrase for 'I need a break'? When your partner says they're overwhelmed, can you let them go without pursuing? What helps your body come back down when you're flooded?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Beck's classic cognitive distortion. Mind reading says: I know what you're thinking, and it's bad. The brain fills in gaps with assumptions — usually negative ones. In relationships, mind reading replaces curiosity with certainty: instead of 'I wonder what they meant,' it's 'I KNOW what they meant.' Attribution theory: we attribute our own behavior to circumstances but others' behavior to character. 'I snapped because I'm stressed; you snapped because you're mean.' Mind reading is the thought that kills questions.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] The most common cognitive distortion in couples. 'She gave me that look — she thinks I'm useless.' 'He didn't text back — he doesn't care.' Both partners are CERTAIN they know what the other is thinking, and neither checks. Gottman: in distressed couples, mind reading is almost always negative. In happy couples, they give the benefit of the doubt (positive mind reading: 'She's probably just tired'). The fix isn't mind reading LESS — it's asking MORE. 'What were you actually thinking when...?'
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Mind reading as conviction: 'I know you better than you know yourself.' The partner who won't accept corrections to their assumptions — 'Don't tell me you weren't thinking that.' Mind reading projected from the past: seeing your mother/father's contempt in your partner's neutral face. Mind reading as control: 'I already know what you'll say, so why bother talking?' The thought that makes the other person invisible behind your projection of them.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] The recognition that you DON'T know what your partner is thinking. The question: 'What's actually going on for you right now?' Fonagy's mentalization: holding your model of the other's mind as a HYPOTHESIS, not a fact. Curiosity instead of certainty. The couple who can say 'I think I'm mind reading right now — can you tell me what's actually happening?' has interrupted the distortion at the source.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] Mind reading happens fast — often before conscious awareness. The body responds to the ASSUMPTION, not reality: if you assume your partner is angry, your body goes into threat mode. Tension rises. Jaw clenches. The body prepares for the conflict it just INVENTED. Noticing body tension can be the first clue that you're mind reading: 'Why am I bracing? Did they actually say something, or did I just assume?'
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] What are you assuming your partner is thinking right now? When was the last time you checked instead of assumed? Do your assumptions tend to be negative? What would happen if you said 'I noticed I'm making up a story about what you're thinking — can you tell me?'? Whose voice does your mind reading sound like — your partner's or someone older?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Beck's magnification distortion taken to its extreme. Catastrophizing says: this small thing means EVERYTHING is falling apart. The cognitive leap from 'we had a bad argument' to 'we're going to divorce.' The mind races to the worst possible outcome and treats it as inevitable. Ellis: 'awfulizing' — transforming difficulty into catastrophe. In relationships, catastrophizing turns every rupture into a potential ending, every mistake into proof of fundamental failure.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] The catastrophizing partner lives in perpetual relationship anxiety: a forgotten anniversary becomes 'you don't love me,' a tense evening becomes 'we're falling apart.' Gottman: catastrophizing is related to the 'Story of Us' — when the narrative shifts from 'we had a rough patch' to 'our relationship is doomed,' catastrophizing has rewritten the couple's history. The partner who receives catastrophizing feels helpless: no reassurance is enough because the fear isn't about THIS incident, it's about the abyss underneath.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Catastrophizing as self-fulfilling prophecy: if you treat every argument as the beginning of the end, eventually your partner stops trying. The exhaustion of constant crisis. Catastrophizing as manipulation: 'If you leave me, I'll die' — the catastrophic threat that holds the partner hostage. Also: the person who catastrophizes about the relationship but won't engage in repair — they predict disaster but don't prevent it.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] The reality check: 'Is this a catastrophe, or is this a problem I can address?' Beck: asking 'What's the evidence?' — the most powerful CBT intervention. Linehan: check the facts. The couple who can say 'I notice I'm catastrophizing right now' has already stepped outside the distortion. Scaling questions: 'On a scale of 1-10, how bad is this really?' Most relationship problems are 3s that catastrophizing inflates to 9s.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] The body responds to catastrophizing AS IF the catastrophe is happening now. Heart races. Breathing becomes rapid and shallow. Panic symptoms: chest tightness, dizziness, nausea. The amygdala doesn't distinguish between imagined catastrophe and real danger — the threat response is identical. The body of a catastrophizer during a minor argument is indistinguishable from the body of someone in genuine crisis.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] When your partner does something hurtful, where does your mind go — to this specific incident or to 'this means everything'? How often does the catastrophe actually happen? What would it mean to treat this problem as a problem, not an apocalypse? Can you name what you're actually afraid of underneath the catastrophizing?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Beck's all-or-nothing distortion. Black and white thinking says: it's either perfect or it's ruined. No middle ground, no nuance, no 'good enough.' Linehan's dialectical philosophy directly opposes this: truth is almost always BOTH/AND, not either/or. Splitting (Klein): the primitive defense that divides the world into all-good and all-bad. In relationships, this is the 'you always' and 'you never' language — the words that turn a partner into a caricature.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] 'You ALWAYS forget.' 'You NEVER listen.' 'This relationship is either working or it's over.' Black and white thinking eliminates the reality that relationships are complex, imperfect, and fluctuating. Gottman: the shift from specific complaint ('You forgot the groceries today') to global character attack ('You always forget everything') is exactly this distortion in action. The partner who is painted all-bad can't win — any counter-example gets dismissed. Linehan: the antidote is dialectics — holding two truths at once.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Splitting in relationships: idealization followed by devaluation. The partner who is 'the best person ever' becomes 'the worst.' No stable, nuanced view. Borderline patterns (Linehan): the intensity of black-white thinking in emotional dysregulation — the partner IS all-bad in that moment, and no amount of history can modify it. Also: black-white thinking about the SELF in relationships: 'I'm a terrible partner' (all bad) or 'I do everything right' (all good). Both are distortions.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] Linehan's dialectics: 'I love you AND I'm angry with you.' 'You hurt me AND you're a good person.' 'This is hard AND we can handle it.' The capacity to hold contradictions is the cognitive achievement of mature love. The couple who can say 'today was bad but we're okay' has graduated from black-white thinking into the gray where real relationships live. Wise mind: the integration of emotional mind and reasonable mind — neither extreme.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] Black and white thinking creates bodily extremes: either completely activated (rage, panic) or completely shut down (numbness, collapse). No middle arousal state. The body mirrors the cognitive split — it's either ON or OFF. Linehan: learning to notice the body's MIDDLE states (slightly uncomfortable, mildly irritated, somewhat tense) is the somatic antidote to all-or-nothing. The body has a thousand shades of gray; black-white thinking reduces them to two.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] Do you use 'always' and 'never' about your partner? Can you hold two truths at once — 'they hurt me AND they love me'? When did you last see your partner in nuance rather than extremes? What's the gray between 'everything is fine' and 'everything is ruined'? Can you describe your partner using 'and' instead of 'but'?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Ellis's 'musturbation' — the tyranny of should. The Shoulds say: there is a RIGHT way to be a partner, and you/I are failing at it. 'They should know what I need without me asking.' 'I should be over this by now.' 'A good partner should always...' Beck: should statements apply rigid rules to flexible situations. Linehan: should-thinking is the opposite of radical acceptance — it argues with reality instead of responding to it. The Shoulds are inherited: from parents, culture, religion, movies, social media — a collage of expectations that no real human can meet.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] The Shoulds create an invisible rulebook that one partner follows and the other has never read. 'They should KNOW I need comfort right now.' But the partner doesn't know — they're following their OWN rulebook. Gottman: enduring vulnerabilities — each partner's set of Shoulds comes from their family of origin and cultural background. When Shoulds clash ('A husband should be stoic' vs. 'A partner should be emotionally open'), neither partner is wrong — they're operating from different inherited systems.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] The tyranny of Shoulds: chronic disappointment because reality never matches the script. Shoulds directed at the partner become criticism. Shoulds directed at the self become shame. Shoulds that can't flex: the person who would rather be right than happy, who clings to 'the way it should be' while the relationship withers. Also: Shoulds that mask control — 'You should want what I want' dressed up as a moral principle.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] Linehan: replace 'should' with 'I would prefer' or 'I would like.' The shift from demand to request. Ellis: challenge irrational beliefs — 'Where is it written that they MUST know what I need?' The couple who can name their Shoulds out loud ('I realize I have this expectation that...') and then negotiate rather than demand — this is the beginning of a truly shared relationship rather than two people trying to follow conflicting scripts.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] Shoulds create a particular kind of tension: rigid, righteous, held in the jaw and the hands. The posture of someone who is RIGHT. Shallow breathing. The body braces as if holding a position. When Shoulds are directed inward: a sinking feeling, the weight of not measuring up. The body of should-thinking is the body of a judge — elevated, stiff, evaluating. Noticing this posture can be the first clue: 'Am I standing in judgment right now?'
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] What are your top 3 'shoulds' about how a partner should behave? Where did those rules come from? Has your partner ever read YOUR rulebook, or are you expecting them to guess? Can you convert a 'should' into a request? What 'shoulds' do you impose on yourself as a partner?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Beck's personalization distortion. Personalization says: this is about me. Your partner's bad mood, their distraction, their frustration — all interpreted through the lens of 'what did I do?' The self becomes the center of gravity for everything that happens. Related to mind reading but with a specific twist: not just 'I know what they're thinking' but 'what they're thinking is ABOUT ME.' A narcissistic wound at its root — but common in anxious attachment, not just narcissism.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] The personalizing partner takes everything personally: 'You're quiet → you're angry at me.' 'You're stressed → I caused it.' 'You snapped → what did I do wrong?' This puts enormous pressure on the other partner, who can't have a private emotional experience without it being interpreted as relational. Johnson: the anxious partner over-reads relational signals because attachment vigilance is on HIGH. Every neutral expression gets scanned for threat. The partner's headache becomes evidence.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] When everything is about you, nothing is about the OTHER person. Personalization paradoxically makes you a worse listener — you're so busy managing your own assumed guilt or offense that you miss what your partner actually needs. Chronic personalization becomes a form of self-centeredness: 'Even your pain is about me.' Also: the reverse — the partner who personalizes NOTHING, who never considers that their behavior affected the other. Both extremes miss the mark.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] The question that breaks personalization: 'Is this actually about me, or is something else going on?' Learning to ask BEFORE reacting. Fonagy: mentalization includes recognizing that other minds have their own weather — your partner can be sad or irritated for reasons that have nothing to do with you. The relief of realizing it's NOT about you. And the maturity of asking 'Is there anything I can do?' instead of 'What did I do?'
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] Personalization triggers an immediate defensive response: stomach drops, chest tightens, the body braces for blame. Even before the partner has spoken, the personalizing body is already preparing for 'it's my fault.' Chronic personalization: a persistent low-grade anxiety, always scanning the partner's face and tone for signs of displeasure. Hyper-attuned to micro-expressions. Exhausting vigilance.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] When your partner is upset, is your first thought 'what did I do'? Can you let your partner have a bad day without making it about you? How much of your partner's emotional life do you assume is about you? What would change if you defaulted to 'this probably isn't about me' instead?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Beck's selective abstraction / mental filter. The Negative Filter says: only the bad counts. A day of good connection, one sharp comment at dinner, and the mind stores ONLY the comment. The positive gets discounted ('That's just normal') while the negative gets amplified ('THIS is what's real'). Gottman: Negative Sentiment Override (NSO) — when the overall sentiment about the relationship is negative, even neutral or positive events get filtered through a negative lens. NSO is the cognitive engine of relationship decline.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] Gottman's research: in happy couples, neutral behaviors are interpreted positively ('They're quiet because they're relaxed'). In distressed couples, the same behavior is interpreted negatively ('They're quiet because they don't want to talk to me'). The Negative Filter rewrites the couple's history: 'It was always bad.' 'They never really cared.' Good memories get eclipsed or reinterpreted. The partner under the Negative Filter can't win — their positives get discounted, their neutrals get negativized, and their negatives get magnified.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] NSO as a prison: once installed, the Negative Filter is self-confirming. The partner tries harder → 'They're just performing.' The partner gives up → 'See, I knew they didn't care.' No data can disconfirm the filter. Depression's negative triad (Beck): negative view of self, world, and future — all of which get projected onto the relationship. Also: the filter applied to the self: 'I'm a bad partner' despite evidence to the contrary.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] Gottman's antidote: building a culture of appreciation. Actively scanning for what's going RIGHT. The gratitude practice: naming three things your partner did well today. Beck: cognitive restructuring — deliberately seeking disconfirming evidence. The shift from NSO to Positive Sentiment Override doesn't happen by arguing — it happens by NOTICING differently. The couple who practices 'I noticed when you...' (positive) is retraining their filter.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] The Negative Filter creates a chronically vigilant body — always scanning for threat, the good moments don't register as relief. Chronic cortisol elevation. The body stays in low-grade threat mode even during objectively positive experiences. The person can't RELAX into good moments because the filter says 'this won't last.' Noticing moments when the body DOES relax — and letting them register — is the somatic antidote.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] When you think about your relationship, what comes to mind first — the good or the bad? Can you name three specific things your partner did well this week? Do you discount the positive ('That's just normal') while amplifying the negative? What would change if you assumed good intent by default?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Beck's emotional reasoning distortion. Emotional reasoning says: I feel it, therefore it's true. 'I feel unloved, therefore I AM unloved.' 'I feel like a failure, therefore I AM a failure.' The emotion becomes the evidence. This is the CBT triangle's signature glitch — the feeling vertex overrides the thinking vertex and presents itself as fact. Linehan: emotion mind treats feelings as facts; reasonable mind dismisses them; wise mind holds both. Emotional reasoning is emotion mind unchecked.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] 'I feel like you don't love me' becomes 'You don't love me.' The partner hears an accusation and defends. But the speaker wasn't making a factual claim — they were reporting an internal state. The translation failure: emotional reasoning conflates 'I feel X' with 'X is true,' and the partner responds to the factual claim while missing the emotional need. Johnson: in EFT, reframing 'you don't care' as 'I FEEL like you don't care, and that scares me' is the softening move that changes everything.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Emotional reasoning as relationship law: when feelings become evidence that can't be challenged. 'I feel betrayed, therefore you betrayed me.' The partner who uses their emotional intensity as proof of the other's wrongdoing. Emotional reasoning blocks repair because no factual correction reaches the feeling: 'I know you said you love me, but I don't FEEL loved, so it doesn't count.' The feeling becomes an unfalsifiable claim.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] Linehan's wise mind: honoring the emotion (it's real, it's valid, it contains information) WITHOUT treating it as a factual claim about external reality. 'I feel unloved' is true as an internal experience AND can coexist with 'my partner does love me.' Beck: 'feelings are not facts' — not to dismiss them, but to create space between the feeling and the conclusion. The couple who can say 'I feel X, and I know it might not be the whole truth' has found the sweet spot.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] The body of emotional reasoning is CONVICTION: the emotion feels so real, so visceral, so overwhelming that questioning it feels impossible. The gut says 'this is true' and the brain goes along. Tight chest, certainty in the belly. The body doesn't distinguish between 'I feel threatened' and 'I AM threatened.' Learning to say 'my body is telling me X — let me check if that's accurate' is the embodied version of cognitive restructuring.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] Is there something you FEEL is true about your relationship that might not be factually true? Can you separate 'I feel unloved' from 'I am unloved'? When your partner corrects your assumption, can you let the correction land, or does the feeling override it? What does your emotion NEED (comfort, reassurance, acknowledgment) as opposed to what it CLAIMS (you don't care, this is over)?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Young's abandonment/instability schema. The deepest relationship fear, encoded as a belief: everyone I love will eventually leave. Not a passing thought but an OPERATING SYSTEM — installed in childhood through loss, inconsistency, or emotional unavailability. Bowlby: the insecure-anxious attachment style is organized around this belief. The mind is constantly scanning for signs of impending abandonment: 'They didn't call → they're pulling away → they'll leave → I'll be alone.' The prediction feels like memory.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] The abandonment schema creates the pursuit cycle: hyper-attunement to distance + desperate reaching + escalation when the partner doesn't respond fast enough. Johnson's protest polka: the pursuer's escalation IS the abandonment schema in action — the louder they get, the more terrified they are. Tragically self-fulfilling: the intensity of the pursuit drives the partner away, confirming the belief. The partner who hears 'You're going to leave me, aren't you?' ten times eventually thinks about it.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Testing: 'If they really loved me, they'd stay no matter what I do.' The partner who creates crises to test whether the other will leave — and pushes harder each time. Preemptive abandonment: 'I'll leave you before you leave me.' Clinging: suffocating closeness that allows no space. Also: the schema so deep it can't be reached by reassurance — the partner who hears 'I love you, I'm not going anywhere' and responds 'You say that now.' The reassurance bounces off the belief.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] Johnson: the antidote to the abandonment schema is not reassurance but EARNED SECURITY — repeated experiences of reaching and being met, over time. The schema can update, but slowly, through consistent evidence. The couple who can name it: 'My abandonment fear is activated right now' creates space between the belief and the behavior. The partner who learns to say 'I'm still here' — simply, without defensiveness — is providing the medicine.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] Panic: heart racing, chest gripping, difficulty breathing. The body goes into separation distress — the same system that makes an infant cry when the caregiver leaves. Bowlby: the protest-despair-detachment sequence. In the abandonment schema's grip: clinging behaviors, inability to let the partner leave the room, checking and monitoring. Chronic activation: insomnia, digestive issues, the body never fully resting because 'they might be gone when I wake up.'
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] Do you believe, deep down, that your partner will eventually leave? When they're distant, does your mind jump to 'they're leaving'? How old is this fear — does it predate this relationship? Do you test your partner's commitment? What would 'secure' actually feel like in your body?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Young's defectiveness/shame schema. The core belief: I am fundamentally flawed, and if you saw the real me, you would leave. Not 'I make mistakes' but 'I AM the mistake.' This schema often operates outside awareness — the person doesn't think 'I'm defective,' they just feel perpetually not-good-enough and organize their life around hiding or compensating. Related to shame (the emotion), but this is the BELIEF SYSTEM that shame installs: a cognitive architecture of unworthiness.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] The partner with the defectiveness schema hides their real self — performing competence, attractiveness, independence — because they believe the authentic self is unlovable. Johnson: the withdrawer in EFT often carries this schema: 'If I show you what I really feel, you'll confirm what I already know — I'm not enough.' Intimacy becomes terrifying because intimacy means being SEEN, and being seen means being found out. The partner feels the wall but doesn't understand what's behind it.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Overcompensation: perfectionism, achievement addiction, the partner who is always performing excellence to outrun the feeling of defectiveness. Or: surrender to the schema — 'I'm not worth investing in' — the person who sabotages good things because they don't deserve them. The schema seeks confirmation: the person unconsciously chooses partners or interprets events that prove they're not enough. Critical feedback hits like a death sentence.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] Young's schema therapy: updating the schema through corrective emotional experiences. The moment a partner says 'I see the real you, and you're enough' AND the person lets it in — this is the moment of healing. Not one-time but repeated, layered, gradual. Johnson: the EFT softening where the withdrawer finally says 'I don't show you because I'm afraid I'm not enough' and the partner responds with warmth — this is the schema being renegotiated in real time.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] The body of 'not enough': collapsed posture, difficulty making eye contact, the physical impulse to shrink or hide. Tension from constant performance. The body that can't relax because relaxing means dropping the mask. Chronic fatigue from compensating. The physical sensation of being exposed — a burning in the face, a hollowness in the chest. In the healing moment: the body softens, trembles, the tears come — the relief of being seen and not rejected.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] Do you believe, at the deepest level, that you are enough for your partner? What do you hide? If your partner saw all of you — the parts you perform over — would they stay? Where did 'I'm not enough' come from — can you trace it back? What would it feel like to let yourself be seen, even the parts you're ashamed of?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Young's mistrust/abuse schema. The belief: people who are close to me will eventually hurt, betray, or use me. Installed through experiences of violation — emotional, physical, sexual, or relational. Not paranoia but LEARNED EXPECTATION. Bowlby: disorganized attachment — the caregiver who should provide safety IS the source of danger. The mind learns: closeness = danger. This schema doesn't say 'something bad MIGHT happen' — it says 'something bad WILL happen, because it always does.'
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] The mistrustful partner is always watching for the betrayal. They scan for inconsistencies, test for loyalty, interpret ambiguity as deception. 'You said you'd be home at 6, it's 6:15 — where were you really?' The partner feels surveilled, accused, not trusted — and their frustration looks, to the mistrustful partner, exactly like what a guilty person would do. Johnson: attachment injuries (real betrayals) can install or reactivate the mistrust schema. Once the trust container breaks, every interaction is viewed through the cracked glass.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Hypervigilance as lifestyle: monitoring, checking phones, reading into every text. Preemptive punishment: treating the partner as guilty before they've done anything. The mistrust schema can make the relationship a prison — neither partner is free. Also: the person who mistrusts so deeply they can't bond at all — serial short relationships, keeping everyone at arm's length. Self-fulfilling: the surveillance drives the partner away or underground, producing the deception it feared.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] Trust rebuilt through CONSISTENT, PREDICTABLE care over time. Not big gestures but small reliabilities: calling when you say you will, being where you said you'd be, doing what you promised. Young: schema healing requires 'limited reparenting' — the therapist (or partner) provides the consistent, non-exploitative relationship the person never had. The moment the mistrustful partner realizes they've let their guard down and nothing bad happened — this is the schema beginning to update.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] The mistrustful body is ARMORED: shoulders braced, jaw tight, eyes alert. Always scanning. Startle response heightened. Difficulty relaxing, especially during physical intimacy. Porges: the neuroception system is calibrated for danger — reading threat signals in safe faces. Chronic tension, particularly in the back and neck (guarding the vulnerable places). Sleep light, waking at sounds. The body that can't rest because rest requires trust.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] Do you trust your partner fully — or are you waiting for the betrayal? Is your mistrust about this partner or about someone before them? What would your partner need to do for you to actually feel safe? Can you let your guard down — and what happens when you try? Has your mistrust been confirmed by this partner, or are you running an old program?
