sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Young's self-sacrifice schema. The belief: my needs are less important than my partner's. If I prioritize myself, I'm selfish; if they're unhappy, it's my job to fix it. Often installed in childhood: the parentified child, the caretaker, the good one who earned love through giving. Not genuine generosity (which comes from fullness) but compulsive giving (which comes from fear: 'If I stop giving, they'll stop loving me'). Linehan: self-sacrifice without self-care is a recipe for resentment.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] The self-sacrificing partner appears ideal — always accommodating, never demanding, endlessly patient. But underneath: invisible resentment building brick by brick. 'I do everything and get nothing back.' The partner doesn't know there's a ledger because the self-sacrificer never ASKED for what they needed — they assumed they shouldn't have to. Johnson: the self-sacrificing partner often becomes the pursuer later — the resentment eventually explodes. The partner is blindsided: 'I thought everything was fine.'
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Martyrdom: 'Look how much I sacrifice' — the moral high ground of victimhood. Covert control: 'I gave you everything' creates an unpayable debt. The self-sacrificer who can't receive: deflecting compliments, refusing help, insisting 'I'm fine.' Burnout: the body and spirit depleted from chronic over-giving. The resentment that turns love into accounting. Also: the self-sacrifice that enables the partner's dysfunction — the codependent dance where one gives and the other takes and neither is free.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] The shift from self-sacrifice to CHOSEN generosity: 'I give because I want to, not because I have to.' Linehan's DEAR MAN: learning to express needs clearly and directly. The first 'no' is the most revolutionary act for the self-sacrificer. The couple where both partners can ask AND give without keeping score — where generosity is bilateral and voluntary. The moment the self-sacrificer says 'I need something too' and the partner responds with 'Of course — what do you need?'
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] The self-sacrificer's body is tense from holding others' emotions while suppressing their own. Jaw clenched (holding back needs). Shoulders carrying. The exhaustion that has no clear cause — the body depleted from invisible labor. Somatic complaints: headaches, back pain, immune system issues. The body that has been on duty 24/7 for years. The first sign of the schema: the inability to rest without guilt. Relaxation feels like abandoning your post.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] When was the last time you put your own needs first without guilt? Does your partner know what you need, or do you expect them to guess? Is your giving freely chosen or driven by fear of losing love? What would happen if you said 'I need this'? Are you keeping an invisible ledger of sacrifices?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Bowlby's internal working model: the cognitive map of relationships formed in early attachment. It contains two fundamental beliefs: 'Am I worthy of love?' (model of self) and 'Are others reliable and responsive?' (model of other). These beliefs, formed before language, operate as AUTOMATIC predictions about every close relationship. Secure model: 'I'm lovable AND others are reliable.' Anxious: 'I'm not sure I'm lovable AND others might leave.' Avoidant: 'I'm fine on my own AND others are unreliable.' The map was drawn before you could choose what to draw.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] Every couple is a collision of two internal working models. Partner A's map says 'closeness is safe'; Partner B's map says 'closeness is dangerous.' Neither is wrong — both are accurate descriptions of their EARLY experience. But they're running these old maps on new territory. Johnson: EFT works by updating the internal working model through new relational experiences — repeated cycles of reaching and being met that gradually redraw the map. The map CAN change, but only through EXPERIENCE, not through argument.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] The map that won't update: 'This relationship is different' doesn't register because the model says 'all relationships end the same way.' Confirmation bias in service of the model: seeking, finding, and remembering evidence that confirms the old map. The person who unconsciously recreates their family of origin dynamics: choosing partners who confirm what they already believe about love. The shadow of the internal working model is that it's INVISIBLE — most people don't know they're running one.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] The map is drawn in pencil, not ink — it can be redrawn. Bowlby called this 'earned security': the person who had insecure attachment but, through therapy, good relationships, or self-awareness, developed a coherent narrative and a secure model. The couple who can say 'My map says X, but this relationship is teaching me Y' is in the process of updating. Fonagy: mentalization — the ability to reflect on your own internal working model — is itself a sign of security.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] The internal working model lives in the BODY before it lives in the mind. The infant's body learns: 'When I cry, someone comes' (safe) or 'When I cry, nothing happens' (unsafe). These body memories persist: the adult who tenses when their partner moves close (avoidant body map), the adult who panics when their partner moves away (anxious body map). Porges: the neuroception system IS the body's internal working model — scanning for safety or danger before conscious awareness.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] What did your earliest relationships teach you about love? Is your model of yourself 'worthy of love' or 'not enough'? Is your model of others 'reliable' or 'unreliable'? How does your map differ from your partner's? What experience in THIS relationship is redrawing your old map?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Narrative therapy meets Gottman. The Story I Tell Myself is the running narrative about the relationship: who's the hero, who's the villain, what's the plot? Gottman: the 'Story of Us' is one of the most powerful predictors of relationship health. Happy couples tell a 'glorifying the struggle' narrative — 'We went through hard times but we came through.' Distressed couples tell a 'chaos and disappointment' narrative — 'It was always hard, they never really understood me.' The story shapes what we remember, what we notice, and what we expect.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] Each partner carries their own story of the relationship, and the stories may not match. One partner's 'we've been struggling' is the other's 'I thought things were fine.' Gottman: when the Story of Us goes negative, it retroactively rewrites history — 'Our wedding was actually stressful' replaces 'Our wedding was beautiful.' The narrative rewrites MEMORY. In therapy, asking 'How did you two meet?' and hearing the TONE of the answer is diagnostic. Warmth = the story is still positive. Flatness or bitterness = the story has turned.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] The fixed narrative: 'They're the problem.' The story that assigns permanent roles — victim/perpetrator, giver/taker, the one who cares/the one who doesn't. Once the story solidifies, new information gets absorbed into the existing plot. Gottman: negative Story of Us is one of the final stages before divorce. Also: competitive storytelling — 'My version is what really happened' — the fight over whose narrative is real. Neither partner's story is THE truth; both are partial.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] Narrative therapy's key move: 'Is this the story you want to be living?' The story can be REAUTHORED — not by denying what happened but by shifting what it means. The couple who can build a SHARED narrative — 'We both contributed to the problem, and we're both working on the solution' — has found the path forward. Gottman: the 'glorifying the struggle' narrative is the healthiest: 'It was hard AND we handled it.' Not denial, not catastrophizing — a story of resilience.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] Stories live in the body as emotional postures. The 'victim story' body: collapsed, heavy, resigned. The 'warrior story' body: braced, ready for battle. The 'we're in this together' body: open, oriented toward the partner, relaxed shoulders. Changing the story changes the body — and vice versa. When a couple physically turns toward each other while telling their story, the narrative itself begins to shift.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] What's the story you tell yourself about your relationship — right now? Is your partner the hero, the villain, or something more complex? Has the story changed over time? When you tell the story of how you met, what's the tone? Could you and your partner write a story you BOTH recognize?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Linehan's central DBT concept. Wise mind is the synthesis of emotion mind (feeling-driven, impulsive, hot) and reasonable mind (logic-driven, cold, detached). Not a compromise but an INTEGRATION — the place where feeling and thinking overlap. Like the Venn diagram's intersection. Wise mind knows what emotion mind feels AND what reasonable mind calculates, and it holds both. Accessed through stillness, breath, the body's deep knowing. Related to Zen's 'beginner's mind' and contemplative traditions' inner wisdom.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] In conflict, couples oscillate between emotion mind ('I'm furious and I'm going to let you have it') and reasonable mind ('Let's be logical about this'). Neither works alone. Wise mind in relationship: 'I feel hurt AND I can see your perspective. I need to express this AND I can choose how.' Gottman's soft startup is wise mind in action: leading with 'I feel...' (emotion) + 'about...' (specific, reasonable) + 'I need...' (clear request). The couple who can access wise mind during conflict can navigate anything.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Pseudo-wise-mind: using 'I'm being rational' to dismiss emotions (reasonable mind masquerading as wise mind). Or: 'I'm following my heart' when actually being impulsive (emotion mind masquerading as wise mind). The person who uses wise mind language while still being reactive: 'I feel that you're wrong' isn't a feeling, it's a judgment. True wise mind requires genuine stillness — slowing down enough for both systems to integrate. Most people can't access it during conflict without practice.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] The still point. Linehan: wise mind is always there — like a well at the bottom of a turbulent ocean. The surface is churning but the deep water is still. Accessing it: pause, breathe, ask 'What do I know to be true right now?' The couple who can both access wise mind simultaneously — this is the highest level of relational functioning. Not passion-less but passion-with-awareness. Not cold analysis but warm intelligence. The marriage of heart and mind.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] Wise mind has a specific body signature: centered, grounded, breath deep and slow. The body is neither activated (emotion mind) nor stiff (reasonable mind) but SETTLED. Many people locate wise mind in the gut or the center of the chest — a felt sense of 'knowing.' Linehan's wise mind meditation: breathing attention down to the center of the body. The body of wise mind is the body of someone who is fully present — not bracing, not collapsing, just HERE.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] Are you in emotion mind, reasonable mind, or wise mind right now? Can you access both your feelings and your thinking about this situation? What does your gut say — not your fear, not your logic, but your deep knowing? Can you slow down enough to find the still point? What would wise mind say about this conflict if you let it speak?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Fonagy's mentalization: the capacity to understand behavior — your own and others' — in terms of underlying mental states (feelings, beliefs, desires, intentions). Not mind reading (which assumes certainty) but CURIOSITY about the other's inner world. Theory of mind applied to intimate relationships. The question at its heart: 'What might they be feeling/thinking/needing right now, and how might it differ from what I'm feeling/thinking/needing?' The cognitive skill that makes empathy possible.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] Fonagy: when mentalization breaks down, behavior becomes opaque — you see your partner's ACTIONS but can't imagine their REASONS. They snapped at you → 'They're cruel' (not mentalizing) vs. 'I wonder if something happened at work' (mentalizing). In conflict, mentalization is the FIRST thing to go — the couple becomes two nervous systems reacting to each other's surface without any curiosity about depth. The therapist's job is often simply to restore mentalization: 'What do you think was happening for them when they said that?'
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Pseudo-mentalization: 'I know exactly what you're feeling' — certainty disguised as empathy. The person who uses psychological language to control: 'You're projecting your mother onto me.' Weaponized mentalization: using your understanding of the partner's vulnerabilities to hurt them. Also: mentalization collapse — the moment in conflict when the partner becomes a THING rather than a person with an inner world. Dehumanization begins where mentalization ends.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] Genuine curiosity: 'I don't know what you're feeling — can you tell me?' The willingness to hold your model of the other's mind as a HYPOTHESIS and let them correct it. Fonagy: 'marked mirroring' — reflecting back what you perceive in a way that shows you're TRYING to understand, not claiming to already know. The couple who can stay curious about each other — even during conflict — has the fundamental skill for lifelong intimacy. Mentalization is love made cognitive.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] The mentalizing body is oriented toward the other: leaning slightly forward, eyes making contact, facial expression responsive and mobile. The breath matches the other's rhythm (co-regulation). The non-mentalizing body: closed off, looking away, face rigid, not responding to the other's signals. Fonagy: mentalization requires ventral vagal activation — you can't be curious about another mind while in fight/flight. Safety enables mentalization; threat destroys it.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] When your partner does something that upsets you, do you ask 'why?' with genuine curiosity — or do you assume the worst? Can you imagine what your partner's experience of this conflict is like from THEIR side? Do you know what your partner is feeling right now? When was the last time you said 'Help me understand what's going on for you'?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-2: COGNITIONS] Linehan's radical acceptance: the full, complete acceptance of reality as it IS, not as you want it to be. Not approval, not resignation, not 'giving up' — but the cessation of fighting with what is already true. 'This happened. This is where we are. Now what?' Rooted in Zen Buddhism and contemplative practice. The opposite of catastrophizing AND minimizing — just the raw acknowledgment of facts. In relationships: 'My partner is who they are, not who I wish they were. Can I love who's actually here?'
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-2] Radical acceptance in a couple: 'You are not going to become the person I fantasized about. I am not going to become the person you hoped for. Can we love the real people we actually are?' This is not settling — it's ARRIVING. Linehan: suffering = pain × non-acceptance. The couple who fights reality ('If only you were more...') suffers more than the couple who says 'This is us. What can we build from here?' Johnson: radical acceptance of the partner's attachment needs — not trying to change them but MEETING them.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-2] Pseudo-acceptance: resignation, giving up, 'I guess this is as good as it gets.' True radical acceptance is ENERGIZING (clarity about reality enables effective action); false acceptance is DEPLETING (hopelessness wearing a mask). Also: acceptance used to avoid necessary confrontation — 'I accept that they hit me' is not radical acceptance, it's submission. Linehan is clear: acceptance AND change. Not acceptance INSTEAD OF change. You accept reality so you can change it effectively.
sections.Light: [SESSION-2] The freedom of reality: when you stop fighting what is, energy becomes available for what could be. The couple who radically accepts their imperfect relationship — their different attachment styles, their recurring arguments, their limitations — and CHOOSES to work with what they have rather than mourning what they don't. Linehan: the dialectic — 'I accept my partner as they are AND I want growth for both of us.' The serenity prayer in action: accept what you cannot change, change what you can, wisdom to know the difference.
sections.Body: [SESSION-2] The body of radical acceptance: the exhale. The dropping of resistance. Shoulders come down. Jaw unclenches. The belly softens. The fighting posture dissolves — not into collapse but into groundedness. Linehan's 'willing hands' — open palms, relaxed fingers, the body's gesture of acceptance. The opposite of bracing. The breath deepens naturally. The body recognizes what the mind is slow to learn: fighting reality is physically exhausting. Acceptance is rest.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-2] What are you refusing to accept about your partner or your relationship? What would change if you stopped fighting this reality? Can you distinguish between acceptance (clear-eyed acknowledgment) and resignation (giving up)? What would radical acceptance of YOUR imperfections look like? Is there something you need to accept AND change at the same time?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Johnson's core EFT behavior. Pursuing says: I'm reaching for you — come back, respond, see me, tell me I matter. The behavioral expression of anxious attachment: when the bond feels threatened, the pursuer ESCALATES — louder, more intense, more insistent. Not because they want to fight but because they want CONNECTION and volume is the only tool they have. Johnson's protest polka: the pursuer protests the withdrawal, which drives more withdrawal, which drives more pursuit. The most common relational dance.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] What the pursuer does: follows when the partner leaves the room, demands 'we need to talk about this NOW,' raises voice, criticizes (because criticism provokes a response, and ANY response beats silence), sends multiple texts when one goes unanswered. What the pursuer needs: reassurance, responsiveness, evidence that they matter. What the partner sees: attack, demand, pressure, control. The translation gap is devastating: pursuit FEELS like reaching but LOOKS like aggression.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Pursuit that becomes harassment: unable to stop when the partner says 'I need space.' Pursuit as control: 'You don't get to walk away from me.' Escalation without limit: screaming, threatening, following. The pursuer who can't regulate the intensity — who knows they're pushing the partner away and can't stop. Also: pursuit through criticism — using complaint as a substitute for vulnerability because asking directly ('I need you') feels too dangerous.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Johnson: when the pursuer can shift from demanding to requesting, from attacking to revealing — 'I pursue you because I'm terrified of losing you, not because I want to control you' — the cycle breaks. Healthy pursuit: the bid for connection. 'Can we talk?' said softly. 'I miss you' said directly. The pursuer who can pursue with vulnerability rather than volume has transformed the most destructive pattern into the most connective one.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The pursuing body: leaning forward, following, physically approaching. Heart rate elevated. Voice rises. Energy pushes OUTWARD — toward the partner. The body is mobilized for connection but wired for fight: adrenaline, tension, the sympathetic nervous system saying GO. In chronic pursuit: exhaustion from constant reaching, chest tight from constant anxiety, the body that can't rest because the partner might slip further away.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] When your partner withdraws, what do you do? Does your pursuit look like reaching or attacking from your partner's perspective? Can you ask for connection softly, or does it always come out loud? What are you really saying when you pursue — 'Come back' or 'You owe me'? What would happen if you paused instead of pursued?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Johnson's other core EFT behavior. Withdrawing says: I'm shutting down — it's too much, I'll make it worse, I need to disappear. The behavioral expression of avoidant attachment or of overwhelm: when emotions escalate, the withdrawer DE-ESCALATES by leaving — physically or emotionally. Not because they don't care but because they care so much they've been flooded. Johnson: the withdrawer is often the most misunderstood partner — their silence is read as indifference when it's actually self-protection AND a misguided attempt to protect the relationship.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] What the withdrawer does: goes quiet, leaves the room, becomes mono-syllabic, stares at the phone, says 'I'm fine,' goes to the garage/office/bathroom. What the withdrawer needs: safety, de-escalation, the sense that they can engage without being overwhelmed. What the partner sees: abandonment, contempt, 'you don't care enough to fight.' Johnson: the withdrawer's message is 'I'm trying not to make this worse' but the pursuer hears 'You're not worth my engagement.'
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Stonewalling (Gottman): withdrawal that becomes impenetrable — no response at all, a wall of silence. Withdrawal as punishment: the cold shoulder wielded deliberately. The withdrawer who uses 'I need space' as a permanent exit from emotional engagement — never returning, never processing. Emotional ghosting within a relationship. Also: the withdrawer who has withdrawn so completely they no longer feel connected to the relationship at all — present in body, absent in every other way.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Johnson: when the withdrawer can RE-ENGAGE — 'I pulled away because I was overwhelmed, not because I don't care' — the cycle transforms. Gottman: self-soothing as a bridge — 'I need 20 minutes to calm down, then I'll come back.' The withdrawer who learns to say WHERE they're going and WHEN they'll return changes withdrawal from abandonment to self-regulation. The key: the withdrawal becomes temporary and transparent rather than permanent and silent.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The withdrawing body: turns away, shoulders curve inward, eyes drop or go to a screen. Heart rate may be very high (Gottman: male stonewalling often coincides with HR > 100 BPM — they're flooded, not calm). The body is trying to REDUCE arousal: leaving the room IS a regulation strategy. Porges: withdrawal can be sympathetic (agitated escape) or dorsal vagal (collapsed shutdown). The difference matters: one is still activated, the other is shut down.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] When conflict escalates, do you leave — physically or emotionally? Does your partner know why you withdraw, or do they just see you leaving? Can you say 'I'm overwhelmed, I need a break' instead of just disappearing? Do you come BACK after withdrawing? What would safe enough feel like for you to stay in the conversation?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Gottman's foundational concept. A bid is any attempt to connect — a question, a touch, a comment, a look, a sigh, 'Look at that bird.' Bids are the atoms of relationship: tiny, constant, easy to miss. Gottman's research: happy couples turn toward bids 86% of the time; unhappy couples turn toward only 33%. The ratio matters more than grand gestures. A bid says: 'Are you there? Do you see me? Am I worth your attention?' Most bids are so small they're invisible — which is exactly what makes them powerful.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Every day, partners make hundreds of bids — and most go unnoticed until they stop. 'How was your day?' is a bid. A hand on the shoulder is a bid. 'Did you see this article?' is a bid. The partner can turn toward (engage), turn away (ignore), or turn against (respond with irritation). Gottman: the accumulation of these micro-moments determines the relationship's emotional climate. The couple who turns toward each other's bids builds emotional capital that buffers against conflict.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Bids that have stopped: when one partner has made so many unreciprocated bids that they give up. The silence isn't peace — it's the death of reaching. Bids disguised as complaints: 'You never talk to me' is a bid dressed in criticism because the direct bid was ignored too many times. Also: the partner who makes bids only for attention, not for connection — performing needs rather than expressing them. The bid economy going bankrupt.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Gottman: the master skill of healthy relationships is NOTICING bids and RESPONDING to them. Not always with enthusiasm — sometimes just with acknowledgment: 'Hmm, that's nice.' The couple who can recognize 'Look at that sunset' as a bid for shared experience rather than just a weather report. Learning to see bids everywhere — in the offered cup of tea, the shared meme, the 'how did that meeting go?' Each response deposits trust.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] Bids are often physical: a touch, a lean, a look. The bidding body REACHES — subtly, often unconsciously. A hand extended, a body turning toward, eyes seeking. When bids are consistently met: the body relaxes, reaches more freely. When bids are consistently missed: the body stops reaching, arms fold, gaze averts. The posture of a person whose bids have been ignored for years is the posture of someone who has given up asking.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] How many bids did you make today — and how many were received? Do you notice your partner's bids, or do they sometimes seem invisible? When your partner makes a bid, do you turn toward, away, or against? Has either of you stopped bidding? What would it look like to respond to one more bid per day?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Gottman's response to bids. Turning toward says: I see you, I'm here, you have my attention. The simplest and most profound relational act. Not grand romantic gestures but the daily, mundane responsiveness that builds trust: putting down the phone when your partner speaks, saying 'Tell me more,' looking up when they enter the room. Gottman: turning toward is the foundation of trust, intimacy, and passion. It's not about the CONTENT of the response — it's about the FACT of the response.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Turning toward comes in degrees: enthusiastic ('Wow, tell me everything!'), adequate ('That's nice'), and minimal (a nod, a 'hmm'). All count — all are better than turning away. Gottman: even minimal turning toward maintains the bid economy. The couple who turns toward each other's small moments builds an emotional bank account that sustains them through conflict. Active-constructive responding (Gable): the BEST turning toward responds with genuine enthusiasm to the partner's good news.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Performative turning toward: going through the motions without actually being present. The 'uh-huh' while scrolling. The eye contact without engagement. The partner can tell the difference. Also: turning toward as control — 'I pay attention to everything you do' as surveillance rather than care. The important distinction: turning toward in genuine presence vs. turning toward out of obligation or anxiety.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] The couple who has ritualized turning toward: the morning check-in, the end-of-day debrief, the weekly date where phones are away. Gottman: Love Maps — turning toward includes curiosity about the partner's inner world. 'What's on your mind?' as daily practice. The radical simplicity of it: you don't need therapy, you don't need techniques — you need to PUT DOWN THE PHONE and LOOK AT YOUR PARTNER when they speak. Presence is the gift.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The turning-toward body: literally turns. Orients toward the partner. Face opens, eyes engage, body angles toward. The phone goes face-down. The laptop closes. The book marks its place. These micro-movements are registered by the partner's nervous system as SAFETY: 'I am being attended to.' Porges: the social engagement system is activated — facial muscles, middle ear muscles, vocal prosody all shift toward engagement. The body says 'I'm with you.'
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] When your partner bids for your attention, what do you do? Can you put down what you're doing and be fully present? How does your partner know they have your attention? Is there a ritual in your relationship that guarantees turning toward? What gets in the way of your presence?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Gottman's second bid response. Turning away says: I didn't notice, I'm preoccupied, you're not my priority right now. Often unintentional — the partner absorbed in a screen, lost in thought, or simply distracted. But the EFFECT is the same whether intentional or not: the bidding partner's reach meets empty air. Gottman: turning away is more damaging than turning against (hostility) because it communicates 'you don't exist' rather than 'I disagree.' At least hostility acknowledges the other person.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] The phone is the modern turning-away device. 'Look at this' → partner continues scrolling. 'How was your day?' → 'Fine' without looking up. Gottman: chronic turning away erodes trust slowly and invisibly — no dramatic fights, no betrayals, just the gradual accumulation of missed moments until the bidding partner stops reaching. The death of the relationship by a thousand tiny absences. Many couples in therapy are not recovering from a crisis — they're recovering from years of turning away.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Deliberate turning away as punishment: the silent treatment, deliberately ignoring the partner's bids to communicate displeasure. Chronic turning away as lifestyle: the workaholic, the screen-addicted, the partner who is never fully present. The child who learned to turn away from an intrusive parent now turns away from a partner who needs them. Also: turning away from POSITIVE bids — ignoring the partner's joy, good news, or enthusiasm. Gable: passive-destructive responding kills the relationship quietly.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Awareness is the first step: noticing when you've turned away and CORRECTING. 'I'm sorry, I wasn't listening — say that again, I want to hear it.' The repair of turning away is itself a turning toward. Gottman: you don't have to be perfectly attentive — you have to be good at CATCHING your own absences and coming back. The couple who can say 'I notice I've been distracted — let me put this down and be here' has the self-awareness that sustains connection.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The turning-away body: physically oriented elsewhere — toward a screen, a task, a thought. Eyes averted or glazed. Posture closed or angled away. The partner's nervous system reads it instantly: 'They're not here.' The bidding partner's body responds with a micro-deflation — shoulders drop, energy recedes, the half-smile fades. Over time: the bidding partner's body stops orienting toward the turning-away partner. Two bodies in the same room, oriented in different directions.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] How often do you miss your partner's bids — honestly? What distracts you most from being present? Does your partner know when you've turned away, or do they stop noticing? Can you catch yourself turning away and correct in real time? What would your partner say about your presence?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Gottman's repair attempt: any statement or action that prevents negativity from escalating. Repairing says: we went off track — let me try to bring us back. The MOST important relationship skill. Gottman: the difference between happy and unhappy couples isn't whether they have conflict — it's whether they REPAIR. Repair attempts range from humor ('Can we start over?') to direct acknowledgment ('I think I just said something hurtful') to physical de-escalation (a touch, a softer tone). The repair attempt is the relationship's immune system.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Repair requires TWO things: one partner making the attempt AND the other partner RECEIVING it. Gottman: in distressed couples, repair attempts are made but not recognized — the partner is too flooded or too resentful to let the repair land. In healthy couples, even clumsy repair attempts work: 'That came out wrong' → 'I know what you meant.' The generosity to accept an imperfect repair is as important as making one. The repair doesn't have to be perfect — it has to be genuine.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Repair as avoidance: 'I'm sorry, can we just forget about it?' — the apology that skips accountability. Repair as rug-sweeping: smoothing things over without addressing the underlying issue. The partner who 'repairs' with humor every time to avoid the real conversation. Also: repair attempts that are actually blaming: 'I'm sorry you felt that way' (non-apology). Or: the absence of repair — the couple who just moves on, leaving injuries unprocessed, accumulating resentment.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Gottman's repair checklist: 'I'm getting flooded.' 'Can we take a break?' 'I'm sorry for my part in this.' 'Let me try again.' 'I see your point.' 'We're getting off track.' The master move: repair DURING conflict, not days later. The couple who can mid-argument say 'Wait — I'm being defensive and I don't want to be' has the skill that makes everything else possible. Repair is not about being perfect. It's about being WILLING to come back.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The repair body: softening mid-conflict. Voice lowers. Hands open. The body steps back from fight posture — shoulders drop, breathing slows. A hand extended. A slight lean toward. The physical repair: a touch when words have failed, a hug that interrupts the escalation. Porges: the shift from sympathetic (fight) back to ventral vagal (social engagement) during repair is physiologically measurable. The body says 'I choose connection over combat.'
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] When things go wrong, who repairs first — and does that feel fair? Can you make a repair attempt mid-argument, or only after? Does your partner receive your repair attempts? What does repair look like in your relationship — words, touch, humor, time? Is there an unrepaired injury that still needs attention?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Gottman's first horseman externalized. Attacking says: YOU are the problem. Distinct from complaining (which targets behavior: 'You left the dishes out') — attacking targets CHARACTER ('You're lazy and selfish'). The behavioral arm of anger, contempt, or shame. When the inner triangle is: fear → 'they don't care' → ATTACK. The attack is almost always a defense — armor worn over vulnerability. Johnson: every critical pursuer is protecting a soft, scared place they can't show.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Gottman's harsh startup: when a conversation begins with criticism, it will end badly 96% of the time. The attacking partner often doesn't realize they're attacking — to them, they're 'just being honest' or 'expressing their frustration.' But the receiving partner hears character assassination, not feedback. The distinction that changes everything: 'I'm frustrated that the dishes were left out' (complaint, addressable) vs. 'You never help, you're so inconsiderate' (attack, character-level, devastating).
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Verbal abuse: name-calling, belittling, humiliation. Attacking that targets known vulnerabilities — using the partner's insecurities as weapons. The Four Horsemen spiral: criticism → contempt → defensiveness → stonewalling, all originating from the first attack. Character assassination that rewrites the partner's identity: 'You're just like your mother.' Relentless attacking that grinds down the partner's self-esteem over years. The most damaging pattern when it becomes the default.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Gottman's soft startup: 'I feel [emotion] about [specific situation] and I need [request].' Converting the attack into a complaint with an attached need. The partner who catches themselves mid-attack: 'Wait, that came out as criticism — let me try again.' Linehan's DEAR MAN: Describe, Express, Assert, Reinforce — a structured alternative to attacking. The transformation: from 'You're the worst' to 'I'm hurting and I need something from you.'
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The attacking body: voice raised, jaw thrust forward, finger pointing, body leaning in aggressively. Adrenaline-fueled. The fight response fully engaged. Rapid breathing. Flushed face. The body is in COMBAT mode — treating the partner as an adversary, not an ally. Chronic attacking creates a body that is perpetually wound up, perpetually ready for conflict. The partner's body responds with its own fight-or-flight: counter-attack or retreat.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] When you're upset, do you attack the behavior or the person? Can you hear the difference between 'You left the dishes' and 'You're so lazy'? What's underneath your attack — hurt, fear, shame? Can you convert your criticism into a request? What would a soft startup sound like for the thing you're upset about right now?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Gottman's third horseman. Defending says: it's not my fault — I had good reasons, you provoked me, what about what YOU did? The most natural response to perceived attack. Defensiveness is self-protection: the psychological immune system deflecting blame. But Gottman's key insight: defensiveness is really a form of counter-attack — 'The problem isn't me, it's you.' It escalates rather than resolves. The inner triangle: shame → 'I can't be wrong' → DEFEND.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Gottman: defensiveness communicates 'Your complaint has no merit.' The partner who brought the complaint feels dismissed, unheard, invisible — and escalates. Classic defend patterns: 'Yes, but...' (deflection), 'I only did that because YOU...' (counter-blame), 'That's not what happened' (denial), 'What about the time YOU...' (whataboutism). Each one says: I cannot take in your pain because I'm too busy protecting myself. The conversation goes nowhere; both partners feel unheard.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Chronic defensiveness: the person who can never be wrong, who responds to every complaint with counter-complaint. DARVO (Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim and Offender): the defensive pattern that flips the script — the person who caused harm becomes the victim. Defensive righteousness: 'I'm RIGHT' as a permanent position. The shadow of defensiveness is that it blocks all repair — you can't fix what you won't acknowledge. The relationship stagnates around unresolvable complaints.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Gottman's antidote: accepting responsibility — even partial. 'You're right, I did forget. I'm sorry.' The magic words: 'I can see how that felt for you.' Not agreeing with everything but ACKNOWLEDGING the partner's experience. The partner who can lower the shield — even briefly — and say 'Part of that is fair' changes the dynamic instantly. The complaint can finally land. The conversation can finally move forward. Accepting responsibility requires the strength to be imperfect.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The defending body: arms crossed, chin lifted, body pulling back or squaring off. The chest armors. Breath held or shallow. The voice takes on a hard, certain quality. The body is BRACED — protecting the core. In chronic defensiveness: the body carries a permanent brace, always ready for the next accusation. The shoulders ride high. The jaw is set. The physical posture of someone who cannot afford to be wrong because being wrong feels like annihilation.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] When your partner brings a complaint, is your first response to defend or to listen? Can you accept 'part of that is true' without feeling destroyed? What are you protecting when you defend — your innocence or your self-image? What would happen if you just said 'You're right' — even once? Is being right more important to you than being connected?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] The regulation behavior. Soothing says: the nervous system is dysregulated — let me help it come back to baseline. Two forms: SELF-soothing (calming your own activated system) and CO-REGULATION (helping your partner calm theirs). Porges: co-regulation is the ORIGINAL regulation — infants can't self-soothe; they rely on caregivers to regulate. Adults retain this need. Gottman: self-soothing during conflict (the 20-minute break) is essential for staying within the window of tolerance.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] The couple that can soothe each other has the most powerful tool in relationship: the capacity to be each other's nervous system medicine. A hand on the chest. A slower voice. 'We're okay.' Porges: the ventral vagal system is contagious — one calm nervous system can calm another. But: you can't co-regulate someone else if you're dysregulated yourself. The safety instruction applies: put on your own oxygen mask first. Gottman: self-soothe during flooding, THEN return to the partner.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Self-soothing that becomes avoidance: 'I need to calm down' becomes 'I'm never coming back to this conversation.' Soothing that blocks necessary intensity: the partner who de-escalates everything, denying the other the right to be angry. Also: maladaptive self-soothing — alcohol, drugs, food, screens used to manage relational distress. And: the demand for soothing: 'It's YOUR job to make me feel better' — externalizing all regulation responsibility onto the partner.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] The couple who can both self-soothe AND co-regulate: 'I'm going to take some breaths, and then can we try again?' or 'Come here, let me hold you until this passes.' Porges: the prosody of voice, the rhythm of touch, the steady breathing — these are the tools of co-regulation. Linehan: distress tolerance skills (ice water, paced breathing, paired muscle relaxation) for self-soothing during emotional storms. The relationship where both partners are each other's safe harbor.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] Self-soothing: slow breathing (exhale longer than inhale activates parasympathetic), cold water on the face (dive reflex), progressive muscle relaxation, grounding (feet on floor). Co-regulation: synchronized breathing, gentle touch, rhythmic rocking, soft vocal tones. Porges: the vagus nerve responds to all of these — slowing heart rate, reducing cortisol, returning the body to social engagement mode. The body knows how to calm itself; it just needs permission and practice.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] How do you calm yourself when you're activated? Can you soothe your partner, or do you escalate? Do you know what kind of soothing your partner needs — space or closeness? Can you ask for soothing: 'I need you to hold me' or 'I need quiet right now'? What's your body's fastest path back to calm?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] The vulnerability-in-action behavior. Expressing a need says: I can't meet this on my own — I need something from you. Linehan's DEAR MAN: Describe the situation, Express your feelings, Assert your need, Reinforce why it matters. Johnson: the EFT softening IS the expression of underlying need — when the pursuer says 'I need to know I matter to you' instead of 'Why don't you ever pay attention to me?' The need was always there; the delivery determines whether it's received.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Most couple conflicts are about unspoken needs. The fight about dishes is really about 'I need to feel like a partner, not a servant.' The fight about in-laws is really about 'I need you to choose us.' Johnson: beneath every rigid position is a soft need — and the therapy is about helping partners SAY the need directly. Many people can't: they were taught needs are weakness, or they were punished for having needs, or they learned to meet their own needs because no one else would. Expressing a need is an act of trust.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Needs expressed as demands: 'I NEED you to...' with the energy of an ultimatum, not a request. Needs disguised as criticism: 'You never...' when the real message is 'I need...' Needs used as weapons: 'If you loved me, you'd...' Covert needs: the partner who hints but never says, then resents when the hint is missed. Also: the bottomless need — the person whose needs can never be fully met because the need is developmental (filling a childhood wound) rather than relational.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] The clean expression of need: 'I feel lonely, and I need us to have time together this week.' Vulnerable, specific, actionable. Johnson: when a partner expresses their need from a soft, open place — 'I need to know you're there for me' — and the other partner responds with 'I am, I'm right here' — this is the healing event. Linehan: assertiveness is NOT aggression. It's clarity. The person who can say 'I need this' without apologizing, demanding, or manipulating has found the middle path.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] Expressing a need makes the body vulnerable: voice may tremble, eyes might not meet. The chest opens (risk). The hands may gesture openly. There's a quality of reaching — physical and emotional. When the need is received: relief floods the body — shoulders drop, breathing deepens, tears may come. When the need is rejected: the body contracts sharply — a physical flinch, the chest closing, the feeling of having been exposed and left hanging.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] What do you need from your partner that you haven't said out loud? Can you express a need without turning it into a criticism? When you express a need, does your partner receive it? Do you know what your partner needs right now? What makes it hard to say 'I need...'?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] The self-preservation behavior. Setting a boundary says: this is where I end and you begin — I can't/won't go past this line. Not rejection but protection. Linehan: interpersonal effectiveness includes knowing your limits and communicating them clearly. A boundary defines what you WILL and WON'T accept — it's about YOUR behavior ('I will leave the room if the yelling continues'), not about controlling the partner's ('You need to stop yelling'). Healthy boundaries are permeable (flexible) not rigid (wall) or absent (enmeshed).
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Boundaries in relationships are paradoxical: they create closeness by creating safety. The partner who knows 'I won't be yelled at' can relax into the relationship because they trust their own protection. Johnson: differentiation (Bowen) — the capacity to be close WITHOUT losing yourself. The couple where both partners have clear boundaries is a couple where both partners can be fully present. Without boundaries: enmeshment, codependency, the loss of self that kills desire (Perel).
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Boundaries as walls: 'I don't DO vulnerability' is not a boundary, it's avoidance. Boundaries as control: 'You're not allowed to...' is not a boundary, it's a rule imposed on another person. Rigid boundaries: the person who can't flex, can't make exceptions, can't let anyone in. The boundary used to punish: 'Since you did that, I'm cutting you off.' Also: absent boundaries — the person who can't say no, can't protect themselves, can't distinguish their feelings from their partner's.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] The clear, calm boundary: 'I love you, and I can't continue this conversation while we're yelling. I'll be back in 20 minutes.' Linehan's FAST: Fair, no Apologies (for having limits), Stick to values, be Truthful. The boundary that maintains self-respect AND relationship. The partner who hears a boundary as care ('They're taking care of themselves so they can be better with me') rather than rejection. Healthy boundaries ENABLE intimacy by making it safe.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The boundary-setting body: grounded, feet planted, voice steady and low. Not aggressive but FIRM. The body takes up its rightful space — not puffed up, not collapsed, just present. The physical sensation of a boundary: a feeling of 'enough' in the gut or chest. A line the body draws. When someone crosses the boundary: an immediate physical response — tension, heat, the body saying 'no' before the mind catches up. Learning to trust that body 'no' is learning to set boundaries.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] What boundaries do you need in your relationship that you haven't set? Can you say 'no' without guilt? Does your partner respect your boundaries? Are your boundaries rigid walls or healthy limits? What happens in your body when a boundary is crossed?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Winnicott's false self in action. Performing says: I'll show you what I think you want to see, not who I actually am. The behavioral expression of the defectiveness schema: if the real self is unlovable, the performed self might be lovable enough to keep the partner close. Not always conscious — many people don't know they're performing. They've done it so long the performance feels like identity. The mask has melded to the face.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] The performing partner seems 'perfect' — agreeable, pleasant, always saying the right thing. But the partner senses something hollow: 'I don't know who you really are.' Intimacy requires authenticity; performance blocks it. The performer's fear: 'If I stop performing, you'll see the real me and leave.' The paradox: the performance prevents the very connection it's trying to secure. Johnson: the false self is a survival strategy that outlived its purpose — what protected in childhood imprisons in adult love.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] The exhaustion of perpetual performance: burnout, depression, the loss of authentic self. The performer who no longer knows what they actually feel because every emotion has been filtered through 'what should I feel?' People-pleasing as self-erasure. Also: the partner who discovers the performance and feels betrayed: 'I fell in love with someone who doesn't exist.' The collapse of the performance: when the performer can't keep it up and the 'real self' emerges — often in crisis.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Winnicott's true self emerging: the terrifying, liberating moment of being real. 'I've been pretending to be fine, but I'm not fine.' The partner who receives this revelation with 'THERE you are — I've been waiting for you' provides the corrective experience. Rogers: unconditional positive regard — the conditions under which the false self can safely dissolve. The couple where both partners can be ugly, messy, imperfect, and real — this is intimacy.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The performing body: held, controlled, deliberate. The smile that doesn't reach the eyes. The pleasant voice that has no bass notes. The body that is ALWAYS on — never sloppy, never raw, never unguarded. Chronic tension from maintaining the facade. The physical relief when the performance drops: sudden exhaustion, tears, the body finally letting go. The body of the false self vs. the body of the true self: rigid vs. fluid, curated vs. spontaneous.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] Who are you performing for in your relationship — and do they know it's a performance? When did you start performing? What would happen if you let the mask slip? Does your partner know the real you, or just the curated version? What part of yourself are you most afraid to show?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] The trust-breaking behavior. Rupturing says: I just did something that broke the safety between us. A rupture can be small (a cutting remark, a forgotten promise) or large (an affair, a betrayal of confidence, violence). Johnson: attachment injuries are ruptures that shatter the couple's sense of safety at critical moments — when I needed you most, you weren't there (or worse, you were the one who hurt me). Not all ruptures are intentional — but all must be addressed.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Rupture without repair is the pathway to relationship death. But here's the hopeful truth: rupture WITH repair can make a relationship STRONGER than it was before (Tronick: the repair of disruptions IS how secure attachment forms). The couple who has survived a rupture and genuinely repaired has proven to each other: 'We can handle the worst.' Johnson: processing attachment injuries requires the offending partner to fully acknowledge the hurt, take responsibility, and be accessible to the wounded partner's pain.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Unrepaired ruptures: the injury that is never acknowledged, never processed, and never healed — it becomes a permanent wound that distorts everything. Repeated ruptures without repair: the partner learns 'I am not safe here.' Rupture blamed on the victim: 'If you hadn't made me angry, I wouldn't have...' The cycle of rupture and love-bombing: the abuse pattern where the rupture is followed by intense remorse and affection but no actual change.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Tronick's rupture-and-repair cycle: the natural rhythm of ALL relationships. We rupture — we miss each other, we hurt each other, we fail each other. The question is not 'can we avoid rupture?' (we can't) but 'can we repair?' Johnson: the Hold Me Tight conversation — the wounded partner shares the impact of the injury, the other partner listens without defending, takes responsibility, and offers comfort. The repaired rupture becomes a story of resilience rather than a scar.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The rupture moment: a physical jolt — the stomach drops, the breath catches, the body freezes. The shocked nervous system. In the aftermath: hypervigilance (scanning for more danger), insomnia, physical tension. Johnson: attachment injuries are stored in the body as trauma — the body REMEMBERS the moment of rupture and re-activates when similar conditions arise. The body of a healed rupture: stillness, softness, the ability to be near the partner without bracing.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] Is there an unrepaired rupture in your relationship right now? When did it happen, and what did it break? Did your partner acknowledge the hurt, or was it swept under the rug? Can you describe the rupture to your partner without attacking? What would genuine repair look like for this specific wound?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Porges' freeze response: the most primitive survival behavior. Freezing says: I can't fight and I can't flee — so I'll stop. Dorsal vagal immobilization: the nervous system's last resort when the threat is overwhelming and inescapable. In relationships, freezing often occurs during intense conflict, unexpected aggression, or reactivation of old trauma. The frozen person appears 'checked out' — blank stare, monosyllabic responses, the lights on but nobody home. Not a choice but a biological override.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] The partner who freezes during conflict is often accused of 'not caring' or 'refusing to engage.' But freezing is the OPPOSITE of indifference — it's the sign that the person's system is so overwhelmed it shut down. Johnson: the frozen partner needs SAFETY first, engagement second. Approaching them with more intensity drives them deeper into freeze. Porges: the path out of freeze goes THROUGH sympathetic activation (some movement, some energy) before reaching ventral vagal (social engagement). You can't go directly from freeze to connection.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Chronic freeze: the person who freezes in every conflict, every emotional demand, every moment of intimacy. The dissociative pattern — present in body, absent in every other way. Freeze that becomes the partner's identity: 'They never respond, they never engage.' The relationship with a chronically frozen partner becomes desperately lonely. Also: freeze as a trauma response that gets triggered by the partner's intensity — the partner unknowingly reactivates old, overwhelmed patterns.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Understanding freeze as BIOLOGY, not choice: this removes blame and opens compassion. The partner who can recognize 'They're frozen, not cold' and respond with gentleness rather than escalation. Porges: the gentle activation that coaxes the nervous system out of freeze — a warm voice, rhythmic movement, the offer of co-regulation without demand. Levine (Somatic Experiencing): letting the body complete the interrupted survival response. The frozen energy has somewhere to go.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] Total stillness. Eyes glaze or stare. Muscles rigid or limp (two types of freeze: tonic immobility = rigid, collapsed immobility = limp). Heart rate drops. Breathing becomes very shallow. Skin may pale or go cold. The feeling of being outside the body, watching from a distance. Porges: dorsal vagal activation — the metabolic conservation response. The body is playing dead because that's the only option left. When the freeze lifts: trembling, tears, deep breaths — the body releasing stored survival energy.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] Do you freeze during conflict — and does your partner know that's what's happening? What triggers your freeze? Can you signal to your partner that you're frozen, not choosing silence? What helps you come back — movement, warmth, gentle voice, time? Is your freeze about this moment, or an old moment?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Jung's shadow projection. Projecting says: what I can't accept in myself, I see in you. The behavioral expression of disowned aspects of the psyche. Classic Jungian defense: the parts of yourself that you've rejected (anger, selfishness, weakness, desire) get PROJECTED onto the partner, who then carries them for you. 'You're the angry one' (said by someone who can't own their anger). 'You're so controlling' (said by someone who can't face their own need for control). The mirror aimed outward.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Relationships are projection FACTORIES. We fall in love partly with our projections — seeing in the other what we wish we were or what completes us (Jung's anima/animus). As the relationship deepens, the shadow projections emerge: the partner becomes the screen for everything we can't face. The fight isn't about THEM — it's about the part of ourselves we see in them. Fonagy: projection is the failure of mentalization — attributing our own mental states to the other's mind.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Chronic projection: the person who never owns their own shadow, who is perpetually the victim of the partner's flaws. 'You're the one with the problem.' The irony: the accusation IS the confession. Scapegoating: loading all the relationship's dysfunction onto one partner. Projection combined with contempt: 'You're pathetic' (said by someone who feels pathetic). Also: mutual projection — both partners projecting their shadows onto each other, creating a hall of mirrors where neither can see clearly.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] Jung: 'Until you make the unconscious conscious, it will direct your life and you will call it fate.' The moment of recognizing your own projection: 'Wait — the thing that infuriates me about you is the thing I can't accept about myself.' This is the beginning of individuation. The couple who can say 'I think I'm projecting' has access to profound self-knowledge. Every trigger is a teacher. Every accusation is an invitation to look inward.
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] Projection triggers a specific body response: the INTENSITY of the reaction is disproportionate to the stimulus. The anger that flares at a 9 when the situation warrants a 3 — that's projection's signature. The body recognizes the disowned part and reacts as if to a threat. Chronic projection: the body tenses in the same places, around the same themes. Noticing the disproportionate body reaction is the fastest route to catching projection in action.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] What drives you craziest about your partner — and is any of it yours? When you accuse your partner of something, could you be projecting? What parts of yourself have you disowned? What attracted you to your partner initially — and is any of that projection too? Can you own your shadow instead of putting it on your partner?

sections.Interpretation: [SESSION-3: BEHAVIORS] Rogers' active listening taken to its relational depth. Really listening says: I am giving you my full attention, without preparing my rebuttal, without planning my defense, without trying to fix. Just RECEIVING what you're saying. Rogers: the conditions for therapeutic change — unconditional positive regard, empathic understanding, congruence — all require deep listening first. In relationships, really listening is the rarest and most transformative behavior. Most people listen to respond. Really listening is listening to UNDERSTAND.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-3] Gottman: the Aftermath of a Fight conversation requires each partner to LISTEN to the other's subjective reality without arguing. Not 'Let me tell you what really happened' but 'Help me understand what it felt like for you.' Johnson: the EFT therapist models deep listening — reflecting emotion, tracking attachment needs, going slow. The couple who can take turns really listening — one speaks, the other receives without defending — can process any conflict. The key: listening is not agreement. You can fully hear your partner's pain without accepting blame.
sections.Shadow: [SESSION-3] Pseudo-listening: making the sounds of attention while internally preparing your counter-argument. Weaponized listening: cataloguing the partner's vulnerabilities for future use. Listening as therapy: one partner always in the listener role, never expressing their own needs. The exhaustion of being the perpetual listener. Also: the inability to listen — the person so activated, so defensive, or so self-focused that the partner's words literally can't get through.
sections.Light: [SESSION-3] The sacred act of listening: 'Tell me. I'm here. I won't fix, I won't defend, I'll just hear you.' Rogers: when a person feels truly heard, something shifts — the defensive posture relaxes, the emotion deepens, the truth underneath emerges. The couple who can really listen to each other has the master key. It costs nothing. It requires no skill except presence. And it changes everything. The most healing sentence: 'I hear you.'
sections.Body: [SESSION-3] The listening body: still, oriented toward the speaker, breath synchronized. Eyes engaged. Face responsive — matching the emotional content. No fidgeting. The body of someone who is fully present. Rogers: empathic listening involves the BODY listening — not just the ears but the whole organism attuned to the other. Porges: the middle ear muscles actually tune to the human voice during ventral vagal engagement — the body literally hears BETTER when it feels safe. Listening is a whole-body act.
sections.Questions: [SESSION-3] When your partner speaks, are you listening to understand or listening to reply? Can you repeat back what your partner just said — accurately? When was the last time you listened without fixing, defending, or advising? Does your partner feel heard by you? What makes it hard to really listen — fear, impatience, activation?

sections.About: [SESSION-4: L2 TRIANGLES] The classic anxious-attachment loop: FEEL fear of abandonment → THINK 'they'll leave' → DO pursue harder. The three vertices reinforce each other: pursuit drives the partner away, which confirms the abandonment belief, which intensifies the fear. Johnson's protest polka at its core. Bowlby: the hyperactivation strategy — when the attachment system is activated and the figure is unavailable, ESCALATE the signal. This triangle runs the pursuing partner in most distressed couples.
sections.The Loop: [SESSION-4] Fear activates → the mind scans for evidence of impending abandonment → finds it (they didn't text, they seemed distant, they sighed) → the body mobilizes → pursuit behavior: calling, texting, demanding, criticizing, following. The partner responds with overwhelm or withdrawal. The withdrawal is CONFIRMATION: 'See? They ARE leaving.' Fear intensifies. The loop accelerates. Each revolution tightens the spiral. The tragedy: the pursuer's love looks like aggression from the outside.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-4] This triangle interlocks with the Avoidant Triangle: one partner pursues while the other withdraws, each triggering the other's triangle. Johnson: the demand-withdraw cycle is the most common presenting problem in couples therapy. The anxious triangle and the avoidant triangle are two gears meshed together — change one and the other shifts. When both partners can see BOTH triangles, blame dissolves into shared understanding.
sections.The Shift: [SESSION-4] Change any vertex: (1) FEEL the fear without acting on it — sit with it, name it, let it pass; (2) THINK 'my fear is about my history, not necessarily about this person' — update the abandonment schema; (3) DO something different — instead of pursuing, express the need softly: 'I'm scared, can you reassure me?' Johnson: the anxious partner's softening is the moment they say 'I pursue because I'm terrified, not because I want to control you.'

sections.About: [SESSION-4: L2 TRIANGLES] The classic avoidant-attachment loop: FEEL overwhelmed → THINK 'I'm not enough / I'll make it worse' → DO withdraw. The deactivation strategy: when emotions escalate, the system shuts down to conserve. Bowlby: if escalating the signal (pursuit) didn't work in childhood, the child learns to SUPPRESS it — appear self-sufficient, don't show need. This triangle runs the withdrawing partner in most distressed couples. Not indifference but PROTECTION.
sections.The Loop: [SESSION-4] Emotion rises → the defectiveness schema activates: 'I don't know how to do this, I'll fail at it, I'll make it worse' → the body shuts down → withdrawal behavior: going quiet, leaving the room, going numb, retreating to work/screen/task. The partner pursues harder. Pursuit feels like confirmation: 'See? I AM failing at this.' Overwhelm deepens. Withdrawal deepens. The withdrawer eventually can't feel anything at all — the pilot light that was the last thread of engagement goes out.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-4] The avoidant triangle's deepest tragedy: the withdrawer CARES — often deeply — but their caring is invisible because it lives beneath the shutdown. Johnson: reaching the withdrawer is the therapist's first task in EFT. Under the ice: longing, sadness, sometimes desperate wish to connect. The partner sees only the wall. When the withdrawer finally speaks from behind the wall — 'I withdraw because I'm overwhelmed, not because I don't care about you' — the pursuer hears it as a revelation.
sections.The Shift: [SESSION-4] Change any vertex: (1) FEEL the overwhelm and stay — use self-soothing to widen the window of tolerance; (2) THINK 'I am enough to be in this conversation, imperfectly' — challenge the defectiveness schema; (3) DO something different — instead of withdrawing, say 'I'm overwhelmed and I need a moment, but I'm coming back.' Johnson: the withdrawer's re-engagement IS the healing event for the pursuing partner. Even a small step toward counts.

sections.About: [SESSION-4: L2 TRIANGLES] The hiding loop: FEEL shame → THINK 'I am defective, if they see me they'll leave' → DO perform a false self. Winnicott's false self meets Young's defectiveness schema. This triangle traps the person in a cage of their own making: the performance succeeds (the partner stays) which CONFIRMS that the real self isn't enough (because the partner loves the performance, not you). Success reinforces the shame. The mask becomes the person.
sections.The Loop: [SESSION-4] Shame activates → defectiveness belief fires: 'The real me is unlovable' → performance behavior: be perfect, be pleasant, be what they need, never show the messy parts. The partner responds to the performance with love → the person thinks 'They love the mask, not me' → shame deepens → performance intensifies. The exhaustion of chronic performance eventually breaks through: burnout, depression, rage, or a crisis that strips the mask away.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-4] The partner of the performer senses the wall but can't identify it: 'Something's off, I feel like I don't really know you.' The performer hears this as confirmation: 'See? Even my best performance isn't good enough.' The loneliness of both: one performing, the other living with a stranger. Johnson: the EFT task is to reach BEHIND the performance to the shame, and then to share the shame with the partner, and then to let the partner's response update the schema.
sections.The Shift: [SESSION-4] Change any vertex: (1) FEEL the shame and SHARE it — Brown: 'shame cannot survive being spoken'; (2) THINK 'I am human, not defective — imperfection is not the same as unworthiness' — schema update; (3) DO something real instead of performed — one genuine moment, one unscripted response, one 'Actually, I'm not okay.' The partner who receives this authenticity with warmth dissolves the triangle from the outside.

metadata.triangle: shame → if they saw me → perform

sections.About: [SESSION-4: L2 TRIANGLES] The criticism loop: FEEL anger → THINK 'they should know better / they should be different' → DO attack their character. Gottman's first horseman powered by Ellis's shoulds. The most visible triangle in couples conflict — it's loud, it's dramatic, and it's usually the presenting problem in therapy. But it's also the most SURFACE triangle: underneath the anger are softer emotions (hurt, fear, loneliness) that the should-thinking converts into righteous attack.
sections.The Loop: [SESSION-4] Anger fires → the mind generates Shoulds: 'A good partner would...' 'They should have known...' 'How could they...' → the Shoulds provide moral justification → attack: character criticism, blame, 'You always...' 'You never...' The partner defends → defense feels like dismissal → more anger → more Shoulds → more attack. Gottman: the harsh startup predicts the conversation will end badly 96% of the time. The attack-defend spiral is self-sustaining.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-4] This triangle often pairs with the Avoidant Triangle: one partner attacks (anger-attack), the other withdraws (avoidant). Each escalation drives more withdrawal; each withdrawal drives more attack. But the anger-attack triangle can also pair with ITSELF: both partners attacking, each convinced they're right, the argument becoming mutual character assassination. Gottman: when both partners are flooded, the conversation must stop — no productive work is possible.
sections.The Shift: [SESSION-4] Change any vertex: (1) FEEL the anger and look UNDERNEATH it — what's the hurt, fear, or need?; (2) THINK 'I would prefer...' instead of 'They should...' — Ellis's rational restructuring; (3) DO a soft startup instead of an attack: 'I feel frustrated about this specific thing and I need...' Gottman: converting criticism to complaint is the fundamental shift. The anger stays, the delivery transforms.

sections.About: [SESSION-4: L2 TRIANGLES] The martyrdom loop: FEEL resentment → THINK 'their needs come first, I shouldn't complain' → DO keep performing/giving. The silent volcano triangle. Unlike the anger-attack triangle (loud, visible), this one is invisible until it erupts. Young's self-sacrifice schema drives the behavior; the resentment accumulates underground; the performance continues on the surface. The partner often has no idea until the self-sacrificer explodes or leaves.
sections.The Loop: [SESSION-4] Resentment builds → the self-sacrifice schema says 'You can't express this, good partners don't complain, their needs matter more' → performance continues: cooking, cleaning, accommodating, managing, carrying the invisible labor → the lack of reciprocity deepens resentment → the schema suppresses it again. Months or years of this loop create a pressure vessel. When it blows: the eruption is massive and the partner is blindsided. 'Where did THIS come from? I thought everything was fine.'
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-4] This triangle is gendered in many relationships (though not always): one partner — often the one who learned to caretake — carries the emotional and domestic labor while the other partner benefits without realizing the cost. The self-sacrificer's performance is SO convincing that the partner genuinely doesn't see the inequality. This isn't malice — it's two schemas interacting: one person can't stop giving, the other can't see what's not being asked for.
sections.The Shift: [SESSION-4] Change any vertex: (1) FEEL the resentment and HONOR it — it's valid information about unmet needs; (2) THINK 'My needs matter equally. Expressing them is not selfish, it's necessary' — challenge the self-sacrifice schema; (3) DO ask for what you need — Linehan's DEAR MAN. The first 'no' is the breakthrough. The first 'I need help with this' is revolutionary. The partner who can hear the need and step up transforms the dynamic.

metadata.triangle: resentment → their needs first → keep performing

sections.About: [SESSION-4: L2 TRIANGLES] The surveillance loop: FEEL jealousy → THINK 'they'll hurt/betray me' → DO pursue/check/monitor. Young's mistrust schema meets attachment jealousy. This triangle can emerge after a real betrayal (an affair, a discovered lie) or from an unhealed history (childhood abuse, past relationship betrayals). The distinction matters: post-betrayal jealousy is a REASONABLE response; schema-driven jealousy is an old wound running new software.
sections.The Loop: [SESSION-4] Jealousy activates → the mistrust schema scans for evidence: 'Who were you texting? Why were you late? Why did you look at that person?' → pursuit/checking behavior: reading texts, checking social media, interrogating, testing loyalty. The partner feels surveilled and controlled → they become secretive or defensive → secrecy/defensiveness CONFIRMS the mistrust → jealousy intensifies. The monitored partner eventually resents the monitoring so much they create the distance the jealous partner feared.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-4] After a real betrayal, this triangle is part of the healing process — the wounded partner NEEDS to check, verify, and slowly rebuild trust. The betraying partner must tolerate the surveillance as a natural consequence. Johnson: after attachment injury, the injured partner needs the other to be fully transparent, consistently. But when this triangle runs without a real betrayal — purely from schema — it traps both partners: one in constant suspicion, the other in constant defense.
sections.The Shift: [SESSION-4] Change any vertex: (1) FEEL the jealousy and assess: is this about now or about then? Is there evidence, or is the schema driving?; (2) THINK 'I can ask for reassurance without assuming betrayal' — update the mistrust schema through real-time evidence; (3) DO request transparency rather than surveil — 'I'm feeling insecure, can we talk?' instead of checking the phone. Young: the schema updates through DISCONFIRMING experiences, not through reassurance alone.

sections.About: [SESSION-4: L2 TRIANGLES] The giving-up loop: FEEL loneliness → THINK through the negative filter ('They don't care, nothing will change, what's the point?') → DO turn away. The quiet death of a relationship. Unlike the anxious triangle (which is loud), this triangle is SILENT — the person who has stopped reaching because they've concluded it won't work. Gottman: when bids stop being made, the relationship has entered its most dangerous phase — not conflict, but apathy.
sections.The Loop: [SESSION-4] Loneliness aches → the Negative Filter processes: only the missed bids register, only the absences count, positive moments get discounted → the conclusion forms: 'They don't care. This isn't going to change.' → turning away: stop bidding, stop trying, invest energy elsewhere (work, children, friends, screens). The partner, no longer being bid toward, may not notice — or may feel relief. Both partners turn away. The loneliness becomes the relationship's atmosphere.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-4] This triangle often follows years of the anxious triangle: the pursuer who pursued and pursued and finally STOPPED. The pivot from pursuit to withdrawal is the most dangerous moment — it means the attachment system has switched from protest to detachment (Bowlby's final stage). The partner who was withdrawing may suddenly notice: 'Wait, they're not chasing me anymore.' This can paradoxically trigger the previously avoidant partner's own attachment alarm.
sections.The Shift: [SESSION-4] Change any vertex: (1) FEEL the loneliness and NAME it to the partner: 'I feel alone in this relationship' — the loneliness expressed is already less lonely; (2) THINK 'My Negative Filter may be active — let me look for what IS working' — cognitive restructuring; (3) DO make one bid — small, specific, low-risk — and see what happens. The shift from resignation to one small reach is the beginning of re-engagement. One bid, received, can restart the system.

metadata.triangle: loneliness → they don't care → turn away

sections.About: [SESSION-4: L2 TRIANGLES] The HEALING loop: FEEL vulnerability → THINK from wise mind ('Both my fear and my need are real') → DO express the need directly. This is the triangle that BREAKS the destructive cycles. Johnson's softening event mapped as a triangle. The pursuer stops attacking and says 'I need you.' The withdrawer stops hiding and says 'I'm scared.' Wise mind holds both the feeling AND the awareness. This triangle is what therapy is trying to produce.
sections.The Loop: [SESSION-4] Vulnerability rises → instead of defending against it (the old pattern), wise mind holds it: 'I feel scared AND I know what I need' → the need is expressed directly, from the soft place: 'I need to know you're there for me' or 'I need you to see me' or 'I need us to be okay.' The partner responds to the vulnerability with tenderness (the Tenderness-Listening Triangle activates in the partner). Connection deepens. Security builds. The loop is VIRTUOUS — each revolution strengthens the bond.
sections.In Relation: [SESSION-4] Johnson: this triangle IS the change event in EFT. When the pursuer can say 'I push you because I'm terrified of losing you — what I really need is to know I matter' — the withdrawer hears the NEED instead of the ATTACK. When the withdrawer can say 'I disappear because I feel inadequate — what I really need is to know I won't fail you' — the pursuer hears the VULNERABILITY instead of the INDIFFERENCE. Both partners are running this triangle. Both partners transform.
sections.The Shift: [SESSION-4] This triangle IS the shift — it's the destination, not the departure point.
